data.title: The towns where the taps ran dry
data.description:
On a grey Tuesday in East Grinstead, a little girl runs along the pavement to the redbrick town library. She stands at the automatic glass doors waiting for them to open, but nothing happens. A moment later her mother arrives. The woman looks at the sign that reads “library closed today”, then tells the child she will not be able to borrow any books after all.

Like almost every business or public building in this West Sussex town, the library is shut as a result of South East Water’s failure to keep the taps running.

The company’s bosses might just about be forgiven if this were a one-off. But it is the second officially declared major incident in South East Water territory in as many months, with customers in Tunbridge Wells, Kent, still facing disruption after weeks of problems.

South East has repeatedly fallen short on its most basic duty. In 2023, 6,000 households in Kent and Sussex went without water when supplies at South East ran out. In 2022, thousands of people spent Christmas with no water due to leakages.

The company, which serves 2.3 million customers, is now well on its way to claiming the crown of Britain’s worst utility from basket-case Thames Water, leaving its licence to operate under threat.

Businesses on the brink

Dave Hinton, the chief executive of South East Water, who took home £515,000 last year including a £115,000 bonus, was hauled in front of a parliamentary committee earlier this month. He had been summoned to answer questions on the crisis that hit Tunbridge Wells in December and ruined Christmas for 24,000 homes and businesses.

The spa town made an unlikely backdrop for a water crisis. From the 17th century, Tunbridge Wells’s mineral waters drew bathers and wealth.

But in the 21st century, water dealt an economic blow. Pub and bar owners in Tunbridge Wells told The Telegraph they had lost ruinous amounts of money.

“In four days we lost between £15,000 to £20,000. We are really struggling,” Bianca Omer, the manager of the cocktail bar Rocca said.

The bar survived. But Tudor Price, the chief executive of Kent’s chamber of commerce, believes that pubs, restaurants and bars in Tunbridge Wells “potentially lost about £20m of revenue” as a result of the outage.

Some are now on the brink. With many pubs already threatened by the Chancellor’s business rates raid, lengthy closure may deliver the final blow for some in Tunbridge Wells.

More alarmingly, a kidney treatment centre, 19 care homes, and two hospitals were left without water too.

‘Flying blind’

Debate has raged over whether the crisis in Tunbridge Wells was foreseeable. Hinton, perhaps unsurprisingly, reckons not.

At his parliamentary hearing he told Alistair Carmichael, the no-nonsense MP for Orkney and chairman of the environment select committee, that the whole thing was a bolt from the blue. In fact, because the crisis came as such a shock, Hinton insisted, he’d give himself “eight out of 10 in terms of response”.

The A-grade mark, as Carmichael put it, raised eyebrows among committee members. But when Marcus Rink, the boss of the Drinking Water Inspectorate, delivered his verdict, the benefit of the doubt evaporated.

“It shouldn’t have been a surprise,” Rink said. The water treatment works at nearby Pembury hadn’t just gone down for a few hours – it had been failing for some time, he explained.

“The start of the incident, in the opinion of my inspectors, was Nov 9.” With the Pembury plant the only water treatment works serving Tunbridge Wells, its continued good health is essential to the area.

Rink said South East was “flying blind”. The plant lacked electronic monitoring, relying on manual observations that missed failing filters.

Despite prior regulatory enforcement notices and missing micro-filtration units, the company had ignored weeks of visible deterioration, eventually leading to a totally avoidable system collapse.

The picture for South East became worse as Rink laid out more details. The company had been notified that there were issues with the treatment works a year previously. Testing had been intermittent, and the specialists monitoring the facility had all knocked off on Friday afternoon before returning on Monday morning. Nov 9 was a Sunday.

If MPs have been determined to hold Hinton to account, the same cannot be said of South East Water’s non-executive directors. Other managers have been sent out to face the music, among them a flustered Neil Hudson, South East’s head of water quality.

“What would happen to people if they do drink it?” a local BBC news reporter demanded to know after residents were advised to boil their water before using it.

“Any sort of normal, healthy individual will probably see no impact whatsoever. If anyone drinks the water and starts to feel unwell, our advice would be to speak to your GP,” Hudson said.

Asked by MPs why he had dodged the media, Hinton claimed journalists would not have asked relevant questions. They would have focused on his salary and South East Water’s finances, he complained.

When he finally came out of hiding, Hinton told ITV that he was “an operational CEO” and as such had spent the crisis participating in “traditional CEO activities”.

Henry Tuffnell, the young Labour MP for Pembrokeshire who sits on the environment committee, pointed out that for an “operational CEO” Hinton seemed to be remarkably short of operational detail.

Tuffnell tells The Telegraph: “You can’t say you were all over the detail, and therefore didn’t have time to do any media, if you weren’t over the detail.”

Even as Hinton faced MPs, back in Tunbridge Wells a second major incident was already in the offing. Still recovering from the pre-Christmas outage, locals were cut off again on Saturday.

Round two, according to South East, was due to “recent cold weather and a subsequent breakout of leaks and bursts”. This was no minor blip. Within days, 30,000 homes and businesses across Kent and Sussex had no water at all.

‘We have had enough’

In East Grinstead, four schoolboys kick a yellow football against the wall. They are the only people in the town who seem to think the outage is a good thing. “It’s a day off,” one of them offers cheerfully.

In Waitrose, almost all of the water has gone from the shelves except for a few bottles of San Pellegrino and some strawberry-flavoured Volvic. There was a water station in the car park the previous day, but it closed after 90 minutes.

East Grinstead council said: “We were not contacted directly by South East Water at any stage during the recent water supply disruption in the town. To the best of the council’s understanding, local knowledge was not utilised when setting up the initial water distribution stations.

“The town council regrets that it was not involved from the outset, as early engagement and local knowledge could have helped ensure a smoother, safer, and more coordinated response for the town’s residents.”

Local knowledge is clearly not South East Water’s strong suit. In December the company began its comedy of errors in Tunbridge Wells with the opening of a water station in Tonbridge, seven miles away.

For those affected, it’s hard to see the funny side. Gary, who lives homeless in East Grinstead, and had just two bottles of water left, told The Telegraph he was “scared”. Like many in the town, he wondered: “What is this all about?”

Jonathan Hawker, a Tunbridge Wells resident who set up the campaign group Dry Wells Action, says: “We can’t wash, flush toilets, do the dishes, wash clothes, top up boilers – all the normal stuff you take for granted – because of this abject failure by South East Water.

“We have had enough. We will now fight for our right to water. Tunbridge Wells is no longer disgusted. We are absolutely furious.”

Heavy hitters

South East’s board is now facing serious questions. Chris Train, the chairman, has been no more forthcoming about appearing publicly than his chief executive.

Described on the company’s website as bringing “a lot of experience”, he is also chairman of Sellafield, which in 2024 was fined for serious cyber security failings. The Commons public accounts committee criticised the nuclear power plant last year for “failure, cost-overruns and continuing safety concerns”.

After Hinton’s select committee mauling, Train offered a review by one of the non-executive directors.

Committee chairman Alistair Carmichael responded: “Members of the press and public would be forgiven for seeing Mr Train’s announcement of a further review of the Pembury failure as an attempt to buy themselves time, to hunker down until this storm blows over.

“They also assert that their review will be ‘independent’. It stretches the meaning of ‘independent’ when their review is to be conducted by a member of its board, supported by its staff. What was the investigation done by the Drinking Water Inspectorate if it was not an independent review?”

Hinton’s furious phone call

Desperate Tunbridge Wells residents have called for the Army to be brought in. Though that may sound a little over the top, there is logic to the request.

Hawker says: “The Army are logistics specialists who are used to dealing with challenging situations. We need experts who can manage logistics in emergency situations.”

South East Water officials can’t even manage a basic task like carting bottles of water from one place to another, he argues.

If Hinton has been the baddie, Mike Martin, the Liberal Democrat MP for Tunbridge Wells, has emerged as something of a local hero. He has had his sleeves rolled up throughout the process and repeatedly taken Hinton to task. This resulted in Hinton phoning Martin one evening during the eye of the storm to give him both barrels.

In his evidence to the environment committee, Martin said Hinton had accused him of politicising the crisis, asking: “How can you sleep at night?” The chief executive admitted to Carmichael that the telephone tantrum had not been his “finest moment”.

South East is more used to getting away with it.

As well as the four major shortages customers have endured on Hinton’s watch, South East Water has ranked in the bottom three companies for disruptions every year since Hinton took over. In 2023, it had the dubious accolade of recording the most interruptions and outages in England and Wales.

Its performance is getting worse, not better. In 2025, just 13 of it 38 annual targets were met – a little more than a third. Among the areas where it fell short were leakages, supply interruptions, water taste and odour, customer satisfaction and value for money. The previous two years, South East scored 15 – equivalent to less than 40pc. In 2022, it ranked positively on 24 of the 38 goals.

The company has suggested that its systems would be much better if only it had been able to raise customers’ bills, a familiar refrain in the water industry.

Yet it already has. Bills went up by 20pc just last year, meaning the average customer now pays £294 a year. It is also one of only five companies pushing to lift bills above the figure set by the regulator over the next five years.

Ofwat also gave short shrift to South East’s most recent investment plan. The company’s performance commitments were “of low to moderate ambition and do not go far enough in some cases”, the regulator said last April.

When it comes to “water supply interruptions, per capita consumption, repairs to burst mains and biodiversity”, the targets South East Water proposed “remained among the lowest across the sector”, Ofwat added.

South East responded by accusing the watchdog of being “unfair, disproportionate and punitive”. The company boasts of having “60 teams repairing leaks reactively and 86 leakage technicians and analysts searching for and finding leaks proactively”. More than 25,000 leaks were repaired in 2024, it says.

For any customers who feel they’re not getting value for money, the company points out: “The average drinking water bill now costs 81p per day, less than the cost of a second-class stamp.”

A spokesman said: “Our priority is improving South East Water in the future. We are strategically focused on our business plan and committed to delivering the infrastructure investment needed. This will improve our resilience as we face the challenges of climate change and population growth.”

It adds that Pembury’s water treatment works at the centre of the Tunbridge Wells crisis is due a full upgrade: “In advance of this full refurbishment, a number of improvements and changes are being made. Some of these are complete, and others are still ongoing, all of which are in line with the regulators’ timescales.”

Finances are underwater

South East’s financial performance is no less wretched than its operational performance. The company has failed to post a profit for the last three years, and though pre-tax losses roughly halved to £19.7m last year, they total £130m since 2022.

Weighed down with £1.3bn of borrowings – a figure that has steadily increased every year under Hinton – the cost of servicing those debts regularly eclipses the company’s profits. In the last five years, more than £400m has gone out of the door on debt costs and finance charges alone.

A further £168m has been paid out dividends, £136m of it in the form of a regulator-approved repayment of an internal loan to its parent company in 2020. South East Water is ultimately owned by a consortium comprising Australian and Canadian funds and NatWest’s pension fund.

In the two-year period from 2020 to 2022, the amount that South East paid out in dividends and debt interest surpassed what it spent upgrading infrastructure.

In 2024, the company revealed that it needed further cash to stay afloat. Ten months later, it received a £200m equity injection from shareholders.

Yet none of this has stopped Hinton receiving hundreds of thousands of pounds in pay and bonuses every year. Total earnings since assuming the top job in 2020 stand at an estimated £2.3m. Last year, he was paid a basic salary of £307,000, a £115,000 bonus and received a further £35,000 in pension contributions and other benefits.

A spokesman for South East said: “South East Water remains committed to a remuneration framework that supports a performance culture, and recognises success but does not reward poor performance.

“The bonus scheme is evaluated by the company’s independent non-executive director-led remuneration committee. The executive directors are not part of this committee and have no say in their remuneration.

“We can confirm that no bonus payment was made for operational performance for the year 2024-25. Details of all payments made can be found in our annual report on our website.”

‘Out of control’

Yet pinning the fiasco on a lack of investment when executives’ backs are against the wall doesn’t wash with Tunbridge Wells MP Mike Martin.

“Quite clearly, corporate governance has broken down,” he said. “We are now in a situation where a major public utility is out of control – failing its customers, risking public health and treating Parliament with contempt.”

If the board won’t step up, Martin thinks the utility’s bosses have to get involved. NatWest’s pension scheme owns 25pc of South East Water.

“NatWest Group Pension Fund will use its influence as a minority shareholder to direct South East Water’s board to ensure these issues are fully resolved,” it said in response to a letter from Martin this week.

The regulator has been spurred to action too. On Wednesday evening, the Government finally showed some mettle by calling on Ofwat to review South East Water’s licence to operate.

In an uncharacteristic show of speediness, on Thursday Ofwat announced an investigation into whether the supplier had met its obligations and offered support to those affected. Ofwat sceptics point out the regulator has been investigating South East since 2023.

There are local suspicions that Labour have, to a degree, let the crisis escalate because Tunbridge Wells and East Grinstead will never turn red. Richard Tice, the deputy leader of Reform UK, told The Telegraph that’s overthinking it. He said: “The problem is utter management incompetence at South East Water. My hunch is cock-up, not conspiracy.”

Yet the crisis is ongoing, and the inquisition is just getting started. By the water station at East Grinstead sports club, where the cars are backed up as far as you can see and people are pushing shopping trolleys full of bottles back home, a little boy on a bike asks if you can drink the water from the pond.
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: Florence flats so ugly they’ve triggered a police investigation
data.description:
Not far from the banks of the Arno River, an ultra-modern block of upmarket flats nicknamed the Black Cube casts a gloomy shadow over the butter and cream-coloured villas lining the embankment.

“Brutto [ugly],” says resident Stefano Pieri, shaking his head as he peered across the river at the dark, burnished brass facade of the building in the heart of Florence. “Some might say it is beautiful modern architecture when it stands alone. The problem is it is completely out of context here.”

The building is indeed out of place – so much so it is now subject to a police investigation, dragging in everyone from architects to councillors. Even the city’s nobility have intervened, breaking cover to stop any further developments from spoiling a skyline that has changed little in hundreds of years.

The Black Cube – officially called the Teatro Luxury Apartments – is a stone’s throw from the US embassy and the Santa Maria Novella train station, and includes smart restaurants, spas and underground parking. The modern palazzo, managed by the Star Hotels chain, offers 150 short-term rental units for high-income students and professionals.

Florentine prosecutors are now questioning 12 people who are under investigation for irregularities in the permitting, planning and zoning process that paved the way for the Black Cube to rise high above the former site of the Teatro Comunale. They are trying to trace how the property was decommissioned and sold in 2013 by the then Matteo Renzi city administration.

What they know so far is that the property passed through a state entity then was auctioned off to an Italian investment subsidiary. From there it was sold to a joint venture formed by property giants Blue Noble and Hines, within the Future Living fund managed by London-based Savills. Hines maintains it constructed the building with all the necessary permits in place.

Here in the centre of the Renaissance city of the 14th-century Palazzo Vecchio, Giotto’s Campanile and Brunelleschi’s Dome, residents cannot even change the shade of their window shutters without an extensive bureaucratic permit process.

People are now questioning just how a US property developer was granted permission to build something locals also refer to as The Monster of Corso Italia.

A powerful group of ancient noble families has called for a moratorium on further high-end developments down river from the Black Cube in a former industrial area near the former Leopolda railway station.

In a letter to the mayor, the 16 noble families said: “We must save Florence from any other ‘black cubes’. No more violence toward our city.” Some of the letter’s signatory families have owned property in the city since the Medici era (1434 to 1737).

Convincing them to speak out has been called Operation Blue Blood by local media, and is largely thanks to a lobbying effort by Roberto Budini Gattai, an architect nicknamed the Communist Nobleman, who has been at the forefront of the protests.

According to Italian media, the letter of protest was drafted over an elegant lunch, where the members of the ancient families finally agreed to sign up to the cause. Among the signatories is Claude Marie Agnès Cathérine d’Orléans, 82, a French princess and former Duchess of Aosta, who married Prince Amedeo of Savoy in 1964 and has lived in Florence ever since.

The Princess is not one to mince her words. “The Black Cube and the Social Hub on Viale Belfiore are monstrosities,” she told the Corriere Fiorentino. “I apologise to the architects who designed them, but that’s the way it is. I don’t understand why, when you do something new, you don’t try – I don’t mean copying – but perhaps taking inspiration from older buildings.”

It is the question on everyone’s lips here. “Its obviously not for Florentines,” said Jacopo Palorni, 37, as he walked along the riverbank with his wife and toddler in a stroller.

Palorni and Maria Christina Silvestre are among many residents who have been forced to move out of the historic centre of Florence and find somewhere more affordable on the outskirts. Like many, they feel their concerns about the urban future of their city go mostly unheeded. They said: “When they began construction we sent several emails, but never got any response.”
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: Boohoo races to raise £35m to ease balance sheet pressure
data.description:
Boohoo has unveiled plans to raise £35m and renegotiate loans to ease pressures on its balance sheet.

On Tuesday, the online retailer said it was holding talks with shareholders over a fresh cash-raise, as well as speaking to lenders about loosening debt terms.

The company, which rebranded as Debenhams last year, said the steps would provide it with “greater financial flexibility to deliver its turnaround and associated growth plan”. It is expected to help bring its debt interest costs down around £20m a year.

It comes as part of a drive by bosses to turn around the ailing fashion retailer, which has been struggling with fierce competition from the cheap, fast-fashion rival Shein. It plunged to a £348m loss last year, with sales tumbling almost a fifth to £790m. Dan Finley, the brand’s chief executive, admitted it was an “unacceptable” performance from the group.

More recently, executives have been battling to slash the company’s debt pile amid concerns about the strain on its balance sheet.

It had been planning to sell fashion brand PrettyLittleThing as part of a push to cut debt levels, but pulled the sale late last month, saying the brand’s performance had improved.

However, investors have remained concerned over its balance sheet. On Tuesday morning, shares in Boohoo were down 10pc, bringing them more than a fifth below last year’s levels.

At the end of its last financial year, Boohoo revealed its net assets collapsed to just £3.9m from £280m a year earlier, while net debt stood at £111m at the end of August 2025. Last year, it agreed to a £175m debt package.

Analysts at Peel Hunt said the latest update showed that Boohoo was “clearly bumping up against its debt covenants” on the £175m loan deal.

The arrangement had previously been the subject of a row with Boohoo’s largest shareholder, Frasers, which questioned the loan deal’s high interest rate. The debt carries an interest rate of 7.3pc above the Bank of England base rate.

Writing to Tim Morris, Boohoo’s chairman, last year, Frasers said: “How is it in the best interests of Boohoo, its shareholders and its other stakeholders for so much money to be sucked out of the Boohoo group when cheaper financing was available?”

The criticism follows years of tussles between Boohoo and Mike Ashley’s retail empire, Frasers. Mr Ashley attempted to oust Mahmud Kamani, Boohoo’s co-founder, from the company’s board, as well as two other members of the Boohoo leadership team, last August.

In November, Boohoo blocked Mr Ashley from voting on a £150m bonus scheme for its chief executive, Mr Finley. It claimed that a “major competitor” and a “significant shareholder” had set out to “disrupt Debenhams Group’s growth strategy and operations” by voting against a series of measures at past shareholder meetings.

Frasers is understood not to be blocked from the new fundraising round.

Boohoo said Mr Kamani, its executive vice chairman, would take part in the £35m fundraising, along with Mr Finley, its chief executive.

In an update after confirming the fundraising plans, Boohoo said it had received “indicative support” to raise £24m from investors. “The company will continue speaking to its institutional shareholders over the next few days to gauge further indicative support,” Boohoo said.

Management added that the company expected performance to “materially improve”.
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: Gail’s boss: ‘Mood among employers is darkest I’ve ever seen’
data.description:
The co-owner of Gail’s Bakery has warned the mood of UK employers is the “darkest” he has ever seen as surging minimum wages price young people out of the jobs market.

Luke Johnson, a key financial backer of the chain for over a decade, criticised ministers on Wednesday for making it “more expensive and more risky to employ people” after companies were hit with fresh red tape and wage hikes.

“No one in this Cabinet has ever been in a business, none of them have ever created any jobs, and I’m afraid to say there is a bigger disconnect between this Government and the business people I know than any government I’ve ever come across,” he told the BBC.

Mr Johnson added: “I’ve spent 40 years growing businesses and creating jobs, and the mood amongst employers and those who invest in this country is the darkest I’ve ever seen.”

Mr Johnson, a serial entrepreneur who also previously chaired PizzaExpress, also said he did not believe Labour had “ever listened to business”.

The comments come after figures this week revealed that youth unemployment hit an 11-year high, with almost one in six people aged 18 to 24 currently out of work, according to the Office for National Statistics (ONS).

The alarming figures are said to have prompted a rethink by ministers amid fears recent policies are fuelling a worklessness crisis.

On Tuesday, the Times reported that the Government was reviewing a manifesto pledge to increase the minimum wage for young people to bring it in line with older workers by 2029.

Already, minimum pay for those aged 21 to 22 has increased by 33pc over the past two years. In April, young workers will get another 8.5pc minimum wage increase.

Labour was ‘right to be worried’

However, retail and hospitality bosses have warned this is making it less attractive to hire younger, less experienced staff over more experienced older workers.

Allan Simpson, the chief executive of UKHospitality, said the looming minimum wage increase for younger workers will add “another strain at a moment when youth unemployment is already climbing”.

He said: “These lost opportunities for young people are deeply worrying for an industry that has always given people their first chance in the workplace.”

Mr Simpson welcomed a “more measured” approach to equalising youth and adult age rates, saying: “Slowing the process would help protect entry-level roles and keep pathways into work open for young people.”

Helen Dickinson, the chief executive of the retail industry group the British Retail Consortium, also said the Government was “right to be worried about the impact that equalising national minimum wage too quickly would have on employment”.

She said Labour should review the “cumulative impact” of its policies on the jobs market to make sure it is not “making a fragile labour market worse”.

A government spokesman said: “We’re determined to help young people into work and deliver on our manifesto commitments to make work pay. 

“That’s why we are raising the National Living and Minimum Wage so that low-paid workers are properly rewarded – worth a £1,500 boost to the lowest paid workers.

“We’re also backing businesses to hire young people by fully funding apprenticeships for SMEs [small and medium-sized enterprises] and offering relief on National Insurance contributions for businesses to employ young people.”
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: Business owner left facing bankruptcy over daughter’s £42k TikTok bill
data.description:
The last thing Andrew Alty expected from his family holiday in Marrakech, Morocco was a £42,000 mobile phone bill that nearly bankrupted his curtains business.

But when his daughter used TikTok for a total of eight hours, that’s exactly what he received.

Mr Alty had taken out the contract through his Manchester-based small business. Unbeknown to him, the deal – which he bought via the retailer Currys but was provided by O2 – contained a clause that left data roaming outside Europe uncapped.

In the following weeks, O2 sent two bills that amounted to £42,000 – more than £5,000 per hour of use on the social media app. The bills left Mr Alty stunned, and his five-person business in danger of bankruptcy.

“There’s no way they should be able to charge that,” he said. “They made no effort to inform us, and just allowed the charges to accrue. I don’t understand how they expect any small business to pay that sort of bill.”

After Telegraph Money contacted Currys and O2, the companies agreed to waive the charges.

Data roaming charges can occur when phones connect to a mobile network for internet access outside the UK.

After Brexit, British residents lost the right to free roaming within the EU, but many providers subsequently continued to offer free or low-cost roaming within Europe. But outside the EU these charges can spiral unless a data cap is in place.

These caps are offered by major networks including O2, but Mr Alty’s business contract with O2 included a clause opting out of any data caps.

Mr Alty assumed that the first £22,000 bill from O2 was a mistake.

“I was on my way to the desert. I made multiple attempts to call O2, but there wasn’t much I could do. I could only assume there had been a glitch, or the account had been hacked,” he said.

When he was back in the UK, he finally found some answers – and a new bill for another £20,000.

He added: “It’s taken up such a huge part of my life over the past two months. It’s ridiculous. [The customer service teams] have not been helpful, the calls have just ended in frustration and despair.”

O2 received the most complaints per 100,000 customers, along with Sky Mobile and Three, according to Ofcom’s latest complaints figures from July to September last year. Almost a third – 29pc – of these related to complaints handling.

Mr Alty then contacted the Financial Ombudsman Service (FOS), and complained that the “opt-out of rest-of-world data cap” included in his contract was not explained to him by O2.

The FOS ruled that although O2 provided the mobile service, Currys was responsible for “contract explanations and decisions about spend caps”, and so the FOS was unable to help with a complaint about O2.

Mobile phone provider disputes can be resolved by either the Communication Ombudsman or the Communication & Internet Services Adjudication Scheme, depending on which one the provider belongs to. The FOS handles disputes about mobile phone insurance, but not the providers themselves.

A spokesman for O2 said: “We’re aware of Mr Alty’s complaint, which the ombudsman ruled was a sales process dispute with his account provider Currys. We understand that this is now resolved, with Currys agreeing to waive all charges.”

Currys declined to comment, but told Mr Alty that it had conducted a full internal review. It said the charges were removed “given the scale and circumstances surrounding the case”.
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: Millions of drivers could miss out on car finance payouts
data.description:
Victims of the car finance scandal stand to miss out on hundreds of pounds in compensation under plans that could spare carmakers from paying out.

The Financial Conduct Authority (FCA) said last year that banks and carmakers would likely be on the hook for £11bn under its plans to compensate customers for mis-sold motor finance.

But Britain’s main financial regulator is preparing to backtrack on proposals that would have paid compensation if carmakers failed to disclose to customers that they had an exclusive contract with a lender or gave that lender first right of refusal, according to the Financial Times.

Around 3.2 million motorists were sold contracts where there were these “tied arrangements” in place, according to the FCA. But only loans that were provided by captive lenders – which are carmakers with their own in-house banking arm – would be affected by the exemption.

Roughly £2.1bn of the total car finance bill are related to tied arrangements, with each victim in line for an average of £686 in compensation.

Millions of drivers who signed personal contract purchase (PCP) or hire purchase (HP) contracts between April 2007 and November 2024 could be eligible for compensation because they were not told about commission arrangements or tied arrangements on loans at the point of purchase.

Around 32.5 million agreements were sold in that period, according to the FCA.

The majority of car finance mis-sold complaints concern discretionary commission arrangements, where brokers failed to disclose that they could adjust the customer’s interest rate to obtain higher commission.

Others were not told that a broker’s commission was at least 35pc of the total cost of credit and 10pc of the loan. These two other elements of the compensation scheme would not be affected by the FCA climbdown, according to the Financial Times.

The regulator started a consultation on its redress programme last October, two months after the Supreme Court found that a lender acted unlawfully because of the high, undisclosed commission paid to the broker and the failure to disclose a contractual tie.

Last year, it was revealed that Rachel Reeves had attempted to intervene in the case to protect lenders from paying large compensation bills. Ms Reeves was reportedly concerned that the case could make it harder for drivers to obtain car finance.

Following the Supreme Court’s ruling, carmakers had reportedly lobbied the FCA to exempt captive lenders from tied arrangements compensation.

They had argued that their in-house banking arms had lent at the most attractive rate for new car purchases.

Adrian Dally, of trade body Finance and Leasing Association, said: “We think there was literally no loss to consumers in that segment. Captive finance companies’ aim is to help consumers buy their manufacturer’s cars.

“The best way to get consumers to buy more cars is to have very cheap finance – low interest rates or 0pc interest rates. The captive finance company exists to provide the cheapest possible finance on the market for that car.”

An FCA spokesman said: “We’re carefully considering feedback and decisions on final scheme rules have not been taken.”

The regulator is expected to publish its finalised rules for the scheme within the next six weeks.
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: Britain’s hapless water watchdog is asleep on the job again
data.description:
Just when you thought things couldn’t get any worse for the parched residents of Tunbridge Wells and surrounding towns, here comes Ofwat to the rescue.

With some households in the region facing their second bout of water shortages in the space of a few weeks at the hands of South East Water, some must have been praying for a miracle. Yet the comically late arrival of Britain’s regulator is the equivalent of sending in Dad’s Army.

Responsibility for the fiasco that has unfolded ultimately resides with South East Water’s equally inept, overpaid chief executive Dave Hinton and the board of nodding dog directors who have somehow seen fit to allow him to remain in post.

But for an enforced and humiliating appearance in front of a parliamentary committee of senior MPs, Hinton has been completely missing in action throughout this debacle.

Nor is this catalogue of failures a one-off. Under its current boss, South East Water has a thoroughly woeful track record of failing in its basic duty to keep the taps running for 2.3 million customers in Kent, Sussex, Surrey, Hampshire and Berkshire.

This is the second officially-declared major incident in South East Water territory in as many months, with customers in Tunbridge Wells, Kent still facing disruption after weeks. Thousands of households were also hit by shortages in 2022 and 2023.

As my colleague Patrick Galbraith and I point out in a separate piece for The Telegraph, South East Water is well on its way to claiming the crown of Britain’s worst utility from industry basket case Thames Water, which really does take some doing given its current sorry state.

Mr Hinton should have been shown the door long ago, but he’s being kept in post by the company’s unforgivably meek independent directors, led by equally absentee chairman Chris Train, who knows a thing or two about companies that fall short.

Train is also the chairman of Sellafield, which was fined for serious cybersecurity failings in 2024. The following year, the Commons Public Accounts Committee (Pac) criticised the nuclear power plant for “failure, cost-overruns and continuing safety concerns.”

Yet, the idea that Ofwat’s intervention will make much of a difference really is the stuff of fantasy.

Britain is awash with dire regulators who are meant to protect consumers from cowboy firms. The Financial Conduct Authority was branded “incompetent at best, dishonest at worst”, by MPs and peers in 2024.

Meanwhile, Pac found in 2022 that failures at Ofgem had come “at a considerable cost to billpayers” after the collapse of a staggering 29 energy suppliers in less than 18 months.

The only regulator that has shown some bite is the Competition and Markets Authority. And its reward for standing up for consumers? A thorough defanging at the hands of ministers who claimed it had become an impediment to economic growth.

Otherwise, in a crowded field, Ofwat must be a contender for the most ineffective industry policeman of all time. It is a watchdog that has repeatedly demonstrated little, if any, willingness to properly hold the industry to account, as South East knows only too well from considerable experience.

The vast bulk of Britain’s waterways have become a public health emergency, and water shortages are now commonplace – the result of extreme underinvestment in the country’s decrepit pipes and sewers by an industry that has routinely prioritised generous executive pay and investor dividends.

On every conceivable measure and metric, companies have been failing consumers on a grand scale for years.

Ultimately, the buck stops with greedy, irresponsible suppliers and shareholders. Yet, years of regulatory apathy have allowed something as basic and fundamental as the provision of drinking water to be turned into a giant and lucrative experiment in financial engineering at the expense of customers.

On the occasions when Ofwat does act, it’s like watching a snail wake up from a coma.

South East Water is already the subject of a regulatory investigation – one dating back to 2023 that still hasn’t been resolved – so what chance of a quick verdict?

As Mike Martin, the MP for Tunbridge Wells, points out: “It’s obvious that South East Water has breached the terms of the license.”

In the face of Ofwat’s impotence, is it any wonder the company has been getting away with it for ages?

Four major outages have been racked up on Hinton’s watch, and South East Water has ranked in the bottom three companies for disruptions every year since he took over.

2023 was particularly shocking, with the company recording the most interruptions and outages of any supplier in England and Wales.

Meanwhile, South East has failed to meet more than 40pc of its targets every year for the last three years. Last year, just 13 of its 38 annual targets were hit. Unsurprisingly, Ofwat penalties of a few million pounds here and there have had little effect.

Financially, it is a mess, too. Total losses since 2022 stand at £130m, the result of profits being consistently wiped out by the cost of servicing £1.3bn of borrowings. 

At times, debt interest and dividends have eclipsed the money that has been spent mending leaky pipes and other vital infrastructure.

Hinton, meanwhile, has earned nearly £2.3m since taking charge in 2020 and is in line for a 30pc pay rise, plus a £400,000 long-term “service bonus” regardless of performance. Talk about reward for failure.

It is an untenable situation that has long been crying out for a supervisor with real mettle.

Lynn Parker, senior director for enforcement at Ofwat, is right to say that “the last six weeks have been miserable for businesses and households across Kent and Sussex” – but why did it take so long for Ofwat to do anything?

Even now, it’s hard to avoid the conclusion that Ofwat only acted because Emma Reynolds, the Environment Secretary, had called publicly for it to step in.

While the regulator has dithered, senior MPs, together with Martin, have done a brilliant job of holding Hinton and colleagues to account.

Ofwat, on the other hand, is hard to take seriously. The threat of penalties, including the annulment of South East’s licence, is somewhat hollow to say the least, given the Government’s plans to abolish Ofwat, the Drinking Water Inspectorate and the Environment Agency, and replace it with a single water regulator responsible for the entire water system.

It is a welcomed move. Long-suffering customers deserve better and the disbanding of the current abject regime is a rare opportunity to create a watchdog with genuine clout. Labour cannot afford to get it wrong.
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: Britain risks losing access to the world’s best drugs, warns AstraZeneca
data.description:
NHS patients risk losing access to the world’s best medicines unless the UK spends more on developing new treatments, the head of Britain’s largest drug group has warned.

Sir Pascal Soriot, chief executive of AstraZeneca, said the NHS could be forced to rely on cheaper, older medicines unless the Government changes course and spends more on developing new treatments.

Countries including the UK risked only being “able to afford generics” in the future if they continued to squeeze spending on innovative medicines, he said.

Generics are older drugs which are less cutting-edge, but have fallen off patent and so can be made more cheaply.

Sir Pascal said Britain was “hitting the bottom” in terms of its budget for medicines.

Currently, the UK spends around 9pc of its overall healthcare budget on branded medicines. Of that, only 7pc is spent on innovative medicines. It means the UK significantly lags other G7 countries.

The UK’s expenditure on new innovative medicines currently stands at just 0.28pc of GDP – around a third of the United States’s proportionate spending of 0.78pc of GDP.

“How low can you go?” Sir Pascal said. “Technologies are going to the US and they’re going to China and other parts of the world, so in 15 or 20 years, Europe could easily lose its health sovereignty.”

It will add to pressure on the Government after a wave of drug companies recently scrapped UK investments, citing unattractive NHS clawbacks and access to patients.

AstraZeneca earlier this year paused a £200m investment that would have expanded its research and development operations in Cambridge and drug bosses have argued that the UK is now “uninvestable”.

In September, Lord Vallance, the science minister, admitted that the NHS would have to spend more on new medicines to stop a wave of investment leaving Britain.

However, reports have suggested that the Treasury is reluctant to agree to a larger overall budget, pushing for any increase to be funded out of the existing allotted departmental cash.

Sir Keir Starmer has recently intervened in the row, with plans to overhaul a decades-old formula used to determine whether medicines are deemed value for money. In theory, this could allow the NHS to buy billions of pounds more in new drugs.

However, Sir Pascal said the Government also needed to reduce the size of NHS medicine clawbacks – or drug makers would be forced to pay more in NHS rebates in response to the rising medicine spend.

He said: “Ultimately, if you get a better price and then a year later, a big [rebate] rate, it doesn’t really help anything.”

It follows mounting pressure on Labour to offer a better drug pricing deal to pharmaceutical companies.

Earlier this week, Warren Stephens, the US ambassador, warned that more drug firms “will not only cancel future investments, they will shut down their facilities in the UK” if ministers did not quickly change drug prices.

He said: “This would be a major blow to a country that prides itself, rightly so, on its life sciences sector.”
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: Brace for Trump’s brave new world of 1.7°C global warming
data.description:
If you have stopped paying attention to global warming, you may soon be in for a nasty surprise.

A paper by Columbia University predicts that we will have our first taste of a 1.7C world as soon as next year – a shock big enough to intrude on everybody’s consciousness.

One cause is the reversion from the current cooling weather pattern of La Niña in the South Pacific to the opposite El Niño pattern of cyclical warmth.

A less understood cause is that aerosol pollution has masked the latent heat effect of past CO2 emissions by around 0.5C and possibly more. Carbon lingers in the atmosphere for several hundred years. This aerosol effect is going into rapid reverse as the world cuts toxic particulates from coal plants, industry, cars and shipping.

The Columbia estimate of 1.7C above 1880-1920 levels is at the high end of the scientific spectrum but the Met Office, Europe’s Copernicus service and others also expect a record-shattering year in 2027.

The paper caught my eye because the Columbia team is led by Jim Hansen, the veteran Nasa scientist, whom I interviewed in 1987 after his watershed testimony to Congress on the greenhouse threat.

Every now and then in journalism, you talk to somebody who changes your structural view. He was one of them. He turned me into what is today an endangered species: a green conservative. Don’t all hurl shoes at once.

The warnings of climate scientists have been vindicated over the decades. The last three years have jolted even the most cautious, smashing records faster than models had predicted. A string of events have been “outside the expected envelope” from the melting of the Antarctic ice shelf to marine heatwaves in the North Atlantic.

It is the paradox of our age that anti-green backlash is sweeping the West – though not the East – just as the evidence hardens and as we move closer to runaway feedback loops that ought to scare the wits out of us.

In case you missed it, the Green Alliance has obtained a suppressed report by the Government and the joint intelligence committee that spells out the risks.

“Ecosystem degradation is occurring across all regions. Every critical ecosystem is on a pathway to collapse (irreversible loss of function beyond repair),” said the text.

Last week will surely go down as one of the most astonishing episodes in the long sorry saga of human folly. Donald Trump revoked the bedrock “endangerment finding”, the legal weapon used by the US Environmental Protection Agency to curb greenhouse emissions over the last 17 years.

The president issued an executive order commanding the US armed forces to buy power from coal-fired plants with “long-term power purchase agreements” that will extend beyond his term in office, a method known as “pendulum proofing”.

The US energy department announced in the same week that it would hand out $175m (£129m) to keep six coal plants open under wartime emergency powers. It is the latest tranche of $525m in subsidies intended to relaunch coal, which has long been in steep decline because it cannot compete with gas, solar and wind.

Trump is actively intervening against free market forces with taxpayer money to prop up the dirtiest form of fossil power.

The federal Tennessee Valley Authority said in parallel that it would reverse plans to shut down 12 of the country’s oldest coal plants. Myron Ebell, a White House adviser, said “total victory” is now close at hand.

This self-indulgent, hedonistic nihilism by the world’s hegemonic power is more than a little irritating. America’s annual per capita CO2 emissions are 14.2 tonnes compared to Japan (7.8), Germany (6.8), Italy (5.1), the UK (4.5) and France (4.0). Texas emits six times as much pro rata as France.

It is hard to gauge how much difference Trump’s provocations will make. Some 96pc of extra electricity added in the US last year came from solar and wind – but that is a lag-effect from the Joe Biden and Trump 1.0 eras.

Trump 2.0 is waging all-out war to obstruct and delay 140 gigawatts (GW) of renewable expansion, even where it receives no federal subsidy and even though data centres need all the power they can get.

However, which brave investor is going to build a new coal plant today knowing that a) the Democrats may retake Congress; b) electrotech is getting better and cheaper every year; and c) the volatile public mood could quickly turn against the fossil industry again?

Carbon Brief says the US still has 189 GW of coal plants. Three quarters are more than 40 years old and many are over 50 years old. Not a single plant has been built in 14 years.

The good news is that coal-fired power fell 3pc in India and 1.6pc in China last year, despite brisk growth in electricity demand.

“China makes almost all the windmills and yet I haven’t been able to find any wind farms in China,” said Trump in Davos. “They sell them to the stupid people that buy them but don’t use them themselves.”

He has not looked very hard. The North China Plain as far as Xinjiang is covered with turbines. China added 430 GW of wind and solar last year. Installed capacity has reached 1,800 GW, roughly 60pc of the world total.

For the record, almost none of the wind turbines in Europe and the US was made in China. Trump is confusing wind with solar panels.

Carbon Brief says China’s total emissions have plateaued over the last 15 months and fell 1pc in the final quarter of 2025.

Over half of cars sold in the country last year were electrified and that market is today as large as the US and the EU car markets combined. Electric heavy lorries are next.

China’s electrotech revolution is now the giant defining force of global energy politics. The ripple effects are visible across south-east Asia. The Chinese model is outcompeting America’s fossil model across large parts of the globe.

Europe is wobbling but it has not yet ditched plans to cut coal, gas and oil demand. Trump’s political weaponisation of energy has given the region a very strong incentive to cut even faster.

Alarmism about the climate – and I use the term in a positive sense – rises and falls in cycles. The mood is highly manipulated by vested interests using well-honed “K Street” methods of disinformation but the public also knows perfectly well that climate change is happening and poses an intolerable threat to their children.

At some point there will be a backlash against the backlash and the next one may be even bigger than the Greta Thunberg storm.

I suspect that this is already brewing as the energy stress from Vladimir Putin’s war subsides. All it requires is the catalyst of a climate shocker or two, or three, to recapture the headlines: a 1.7C world in 2027 would surely do it.

My advice to the Tories is listen hard to distant but approaching thunder. Climate negligence and fossil politics will not be a winning formula in the hotter world of the next election. That niche is best left to Reform.

There is a wide-open window for the Conservatives to be the responsible party of nature and free market, winning back the hard-working silent middle as urban Labour lurches further to the welfare Left.

Ecology will soon be fashionable again.

data.title: Taxpayer on the hook for millions as solar company faces administration
data.description:
Taxpayers are facing a potential multimillion-pound blow as a leading British solar energy developer risks the threat of administration.

Hive Energy is preparing to appoint administrators just months after securing a £60m taxpayer-backed loan from HSBC to launch itself as a global operation.

The loan was announced by the UK Government last November at the UN’s COP30 climate conference to show how the UK was supporting the global expansion of solar. The loan guarantee was personally endorsed by Tim Reid, the chief executive of UK Export Finance Agency (UKEF), who said he was “proud” to support Hive.

It is the second such loan offered to Hive Energy, following a similar guarantee for a £19m loan from Santander taken out in 2024.

This week Hive issued a “notice of intention to appoint administrators” with law firm DLA Piper as its legal advisors. The move suggests the company – which has 200 renewables projects in its global pipeline, 15 of which are in the UK – is at risk of collapse.

It also raises the threat that taxpayers could be on the hook for loan repayments, and questions about the quality of UKEF’s vetting of Hive before approving the latest loan.

When asked by The Telegraph if taxpayer money was at risk, the UKEF and Mr Reid declined to comment.

HSBC and Santander banks also both declined to comment.

Hive Energy, which was founded by city entrepreneur and ocean racing yachtsman Giles Redpath, confirmed that it was suffering problems with creditors linked to its subsidiary Ethical Power, which constructs and operates solar and other renewables sites across the UK, Spain, Italy, Greece and New Zealand.

A spokesman said: “This process does not mean that the business has entered administration or that it will enter administration, but rather provides time and stability to explore strategic options in an orderly manner.

“This is not a decision to cease trading, and it does not change our day-to-day operations or the ongoing development of our projects.”

Hampshire-based Hive Energy was founded by Mr Redpath, a former telecoms entrepreneur, in 2010 to exploit the surging interest in solar and other renewable energy.

The latest accounts, covering the year to March 2024, describe Hive as operating in 20 different countries through 180 subsidiary companies – most of which are linked to particular renewables projects.

Its UK projects include the Cleve Hill solar park, the UK’s largest operational solar and battery storage facility on marshes on Kent’s north coast prized by locals as a wildlife refuge.

Mr Redpath is also the driving force behind the $5.8bn (£4.2bn) Hive Hydrogen project in South Africa, as well as similar large-scale hubs in Spain and Chile. These projects aim to produce green ammonia for the global shipping and fertiliser industries.

Piling losses and loans

Company accounts from 2024, the last published, show Hive has two directors but that Mr Redpath has a majority shareholding.

The company made an operating loss of £29m in that year, but sold off assets worth £37m, paying the resulting dividends of about £7.4m to “a director, their wife and children”.

The same accounts state that Hive’s “directors and shareholders” had borrowed a further £7.2m from the company.

Outside the boardroom Mr Redpath is a renowned racing yachtsman, well-known for competing in races around the British Isles and across the Atlantic. In 2023, he commissioned a £5m racing multihull called Jack.

UKEF said last November it had guaranteed the £60m bank loan to Hive to help it scale up solar power projects across Europe, North America, the Caribbean, South America and Africa.

At the time, Mr Reid said: “We’re proud to continue supporting British businesses like Hive Energy, which reinforce the UK’s position as a leader in the global transition to clean energy.”

A Hive spokesman said: “Hive Energy is a long-term investor in the Ethical Power group of companies. That group has been undertaking a significant restructuring process.

“During this period, Hive (which has supported Ethical through both funding and guarantees) has experienced some creditor pressure in relation to several Ethical construction projects, creating a risk of enforcement action.”

The spokesman added: “In light of this, the Board of Hive has filed a Notice of Intention to appoint administrators to … provide a short period of statutory protection and stability.”

“Hive is a long-standing and fundamentally strong business and … continues to trade under the control of its existing Board and management team, as do all companies within the Hive Group.”

Ethical Power said: “Ethical Power is not affected by these actions as Hive Energy is a shareholder and not a parent/holding company. Ethical Power continues its operations, delivering projects and receiving payments from clients.”

data.title: The banks are booming — one guess what Reeves will do next
data.description:
Profits at NatWest and Lloyds are up. Barclays is doing well enough to reward its chief executive with a £15m pay package. Spain’s Santander is making more money than ever from its British operations.

It is great to see at least one sector of the British economy doing well. There is just one problem. As the Government shifts Left, it will be impossible to resist another raid on the sector – even though it will damage the whole economy.

The economy may have stagnated and business confidence may have fallen to record lows, but at least the high street banks are doing better than ever.

NatWest confirmed the revival on Friday when it reported annual profits up by 25pc at £7.7bn, capping an earnings season that has seen all the major British lenders report excellent results.

It is not hard to understand why all the major banks are reporting such strong profits. Interest rates are positive, households are so worried about their finances that savings are surging, and the property market is resilient.

Small businesses may be struggling to cope with a blizzard of tax rises imposed on them by a Government that doesn’t understand how hard it can be to keep a company afloat, but we have not yet seen a full-scale recession or the kind of mass closures that trigger huge losses for the lenders.

Against that backdrop, the banks should be making money. And they are.

The problem is that this is also going to make them an obvious target for the Government.

Over the past week, with Sir Keir Starmer clinging desperately to power, it has become painfully clear that Labour is going to shift significantly to the Left. It has nowhere else to go.

The backbenchers will demand it, and the main challengers to the Prime Minister, from Angela Rayner to Andy Burnham, are all positioned to Sir Keir’s left, while the moderate right of the party has been fatally weakened by its foolish association with Peter Mandelson.

At the same time, Rachel Reeves is still desperate for money, both to keep borrowing under control, and to offer the extra welfare spending and pay rises for the public sector that are the only things that Labour MPs and party activists seem to believe matter.

With the economy stagnating and contracting when measured by GDP per capita, the tax rises she has already imposed are going to raise less revenue than the Treasury forecast.

It is not hard to work out what is going to happen next. By the summer, yet another “black hole” will have opened up in the Government’s finances.

With the manifesto pledge not to raise any of the three main taxes still in place, a stealth tax on business will be one of the few ways to raise money.

The conclusion is that a windfall tax on the banks looks grimly inevitable. Indeed, in the run-up to the last Budget, the trade unions and a string of Left-wing think tanks were already arguing for a raid on the financial sector.

This year, the demands will be even louder. And yet that will turn into one more self-inflicted disaster.

Sure, the banks may appear to be making huge profits.

But that doesn’t mean this is the right time to hit them with taxes on top of the 25pc corporation tax they already pay, the 3pc “surcharge” on profits above £100m, along with all the National Insurance contributions they pay for their staff, the rates owed on their branches, and of course all the taxes paid by the businesses they lend to.

In reality, the banking industry still faces plenty of serious challenges. The app-based banks, such as Monzo, are still growing quickly, and their expansion is a direct challenge to the traditional giants on the high street. Artificial intelligence will only accelerate that.

It will take a lot of investment in technology for the old-school banks to hang on to the customers they already have – never mind find new ones.

Their costs are still far too high, and political opposition to closing expensive branches is intense, and it will only get worse as soaring taxes mean almost every kind of business – from shops to cafés – closes down.

The tech giants such as Apple, Amazon and Meta have not managed to crack the finance market yet, but that does not mean they won’t keep trying, and with payment systems on every smartphone they will keep chipping away at the margin.

Unlike retailing or media, banking has proved surprisingly resilient to digital disruption, but it may only be a matter of time.

Perhaps worst of all, the UK may soon face a major recession if the global economy or the stock market crashes, and with a spectacularly incompetent Government doing everything possible to destroy confidence in Britain’s prospects.

The banks will need plenty of investment to deal with all those challenges and to keep some capital in reserve to ride through a recession.

If the Government decides to confiscate all the spare cash they make in the good years, then it won’t be available when times are tougher.

The bumper profits the high street banks are making this year won’t necessarily last.

In reality, Britain needs to get away from the idea that success always has to be punished with extra taxes, or that we can get richer by confiscating wealth instead of creating it. A healthy economy needs a healthy banking industry.

The Chancellor will come under intense pressure over the next few months to impose a windfall levy on the UK’s finance industry.

The headline numbers may even offer some justification for that, even if it hardly stands up to a moment of serious scrutiny.

However, it would be a serious mistake – and the entire British economy will suffer if it happens.

data.title: Inflation calculator: how the latest rate will affect your savings
data.description:
This calculator shows the current rate of Consumer Prices Index (CPI) inflation will affect the return on your savings.

Inflation calculator

How to use this calculator

Type in the amount you have in a savings account or cash Isa, and the interest rate you’re currently receiving.

Press the “Calculate” button to see the result.

How it works

This calculator works by figuring out how much interest your savings will earn over the course of a year – this assumes interest is paid annually, and the interest rate and amount of savings remains the same.

This is pitted against the most recent CPI measure of inflation, as reported by the Office for National Statistics.

Assuming inflation remains steady, it then shows the value your savings will lose in real terms due to inflationary price increases.

The calculator will also show you what the cumulative impact of these losses will be for each of the next five years.

What is this calculator useful for?

The calculator can help you gauge whether or not it’s worth switching to a savings account or cash Isa offering a higher rate of interest.

This is particularly important when inflation is high, but in general it’s a good idea to make sure you’re taking advantage of the best savings rates to reduce the effect of price increases. If you don’t, put simply, your money won’t buy as much as it could the previous year.

You can also use the calculator to see how much of a difference it would make to switch savings accounts for a better rate.

For example, someone with £20,000 in an account paying today’s average easy-access rate, according to Moneyfacts, of 2.42pc would make a “real terms” loss of £112.62 after a year because of inflation. They would earn £484 in interest, but their pot would effectively be worth just £19,887.38 after a year when adjusted for inflation.

Switching to the top easy-access savings rate which, according to today’s tables showing the best easy access savings accounts, is from Cahoot and pays 5pc, they’d make an inflationary increase of £388.35, based on actual interest of £1,000.

data.title: The auto industry’s gamble on electric cars has turned into a catastrophe
data.description:
We are already getting close to the €100bn (£87bn) mark and the total is still rising.

Stellantis – the company that controls Fiat, Peugeot and Chrysler – announced on Friday that it was writing down €22bn on its electric vehicle unit. The bill is only the latest in a series of enormous losses suffered by the major American and European auto manufacturers who jumped on the EV bandwagon.

In reality, it turns out that electric cars are only a small part of the overall market and that, insofar as it exists, Chinese manufacturers will capture most of the sales.

The gamble on electric cars has turned into a catastrophe and it will be many years before the industry recovers.

With less than four years remaining until the original target date for banning the sale of all new petrol and diesel cars, the giants of the industry were meant to be riding a boom in sales of battery-powered vehicles by now.

Sleek new models would be rolling off the production lines, new battery plants would be creating hundreds of thousands of jobs, while the billions poured into investment would be the catalyst for reindustrialising both Europe and the United States.

“We’re going to need 70,000 skilled people just to make batteries across this country,” announced Boris Johnson, the former prime minister, back in 2021. He promised unlimited government support for British EV production.

Over in France, Emmanuel Macron, the French president, was pouring billions into making his country a force in battery and EV production.

So we are starting to see the results of all that investment, right? Sales are booming, profits are rising and new jobs are being created? Well, not exactly.

Stellantis’s €22bn write-down on Friday, combined with plans to offload a stake in a Canadian battery joint venture, sent shares down 27pc on the day. They have now more than halved over the last year, taking Stellantis’s value down to just €17bn.

Unfortunately, it is far from alone. General Motors said last month that it was taking a $7bn (£5bn) charge against earnings, almost all of which was accounted for by its EV unit.

Just before Christmas, Ford said it was taking a $19.5bn hit on its EV business, including cancelled vehicles and a battery joint venture. Volkswagen has started scaling back production of EVs amid heavy losses.

Porsche, which had said that electric vehicles would account for 80pc of profits by the end of this decade, instead announced a €3.1bn charge late last year after scaling back those targets.

Even Tesla, a company that knows more about electric cars than any of its rivals, is starting to struggle. It has scrapped the iconic Model S and Elon Musk, its chief executive, is desperately trying to convince everyone that robots are the future instead.

Antonio Filosa, the chief executive of Stellantis, conceded that the company had overestimated “the pace of the energy transition that distanced us from many car buyers’ real-world needs, means and desires”.

It is a painful admission but one that is at least honest. One point is surely clear. We are not hearing very much about how the transition to EVs would lead to an industrial renaissance any more.

There have been two major problems. First, EVs may only be a niche product.

Drivers are worried about the range, it is far from clear they are better for the environment once the impact of all the raw materials in the manufacturing process is taken into account, the charging infrastructure is not in place and we don’t generate the electricity to power them all at a price cheap enough to make EVs cost-effective.

Next, where there is a market, the new breed of Chinese brands led by BYD is walking away with it.

The traditional auto giants thought the transition was just a matter of replacing an internal combustion engine with a big battery, but it turned out that an EV was a piece of electronics with wheels attached. It has much more in common with the mobile phone market than anything the petrolheads running the industry were familiar with.

It is far easier to create a new EV company from scratch than to convert one of the traditional giants.

The result? All the auto companies are now in deep trouble.

Even worse, under the direction of Ed Miliband, the fanatical Energy Secretary, Britain is pressing on blindly with the 2030 target for phasing out sales of new petrol cars even as the rest of the world recognises that it is complete madness.

Indeed, only this week, the boss of South Korea’s Hyundai warned that sticking to the target threatened “severe disruption” in the UK auto market.

The blunt truth is this: the massive bet that the auto giants took on EVs has backfired spectacularly.

It has cost them tens of billions in capital, it has wasted five years of time and energy that could have been more profitably deployed elsewhere and – perhaps worst of all – it has allowed Chinese manufacturers that no one had ever heard of to establish new brands. It will very difficult to get rid of them now.

It is one of the worst self-inflicted calamities of recent industrial history.

To start the recovery process, the manufacturers will have to persuade governments to stop imposing ridiculous top-down targets that only distort the market. They will have to ruthlessly get rid of units that don’t make any money. They will have to wean themselves off subsidies and, most crucially, they will need to reconnect with their customers, making cars that people can afford and want to drive again.

After the madness of the last five years, it is the only way back – and it will be a long, hard and expensive road.

data.title: The Cornish mine that could help Trump in his new arms race with China
data.description:
Britain’s last operational tin mine, South Crofty, has been sitting dormant in Cornwall for nearly 30 years. But now the historic site may be revived thanks to an unlikely benefactor: Donald Trump.

The US president’s administration last week said it was willing to loan up to $225m (£165m) to developer Cornish Metals to support the mine’s reopening – so long as it agrees to send some of the output across the Atlantic.

Just two days earlier, and more than 4,600 miles away, a Washington-backed investment fund had announced a deal to acquire a 40pc share in a copper and cobalt mine run by Glencore in the Democratic Republic of Congo.

And in Western Australia, the US government has emerged as one of the backers of a scheme to build a new refinery that will produce gallium, a rare metal.

The investments follow a spate of dealmaking by the Trump administration at home in the past year – which has seen it take stakes in a magnet producer, a lithium mine and rare earth refineries – among other things.

According to experts, they are all examples of the new, more muscular approach Washington is taking to securing critical minerals abroad as it seeks to break China’s stranglehold over global supplies.

Tin, copper, cobalt and gallium are all commonly used in electronics, while lithium – known as “white gold” – is the main ingredient in batteries that power the modern world.

Rare earth magnets, meanwhile, are found in the spinning parts of wind turbines, electric motors and even fighter plane wing flaps.

Without them, and a plethora of other metals, factory production lines across America would grind to a halt.

That briefly became a real prospect last year, when the US was locked in a trade standoff with China.

“One of the challenges we’ve had in the last 20 years in the United States is our mineral security efforts have largely been contained to handshake diplomacy,” says Gracelin Baskaran, a critical minerals expert at the Center for Strategic and International Studies (CSIS).

“But handshake diplomacy doesn’t bring projects online. And it doesn’t yield minerals that can meet critical security interests.

“It’s the start, but what is the support that we need to get projects into production? It’s financing, it’s trade agreements, it’s off-take agreements [where a customer agrees to buy raw material before it is produced]. That is how we increase access to minerals.

“We’re going from paper agreements to deploying capital.”

US trailing China

This is the thinking behind the dizzying series of deals announced by American officials in recent months.

Efforts to start directly nurturing alternative supplies began under Joe Biden but have accelerated under Trump, with investments made through several arms of the state – including the department of war, the department of energy, the Export-Import Bank of the United States (Exim) and the US International Development Finance Corporation (DFC).

The DFC was the investor that supported Glencore’s copper and cobalt operations, while Exim has extended the offer of loans and financing to the tin project being developed by Cornish Metals.

Yet the Trump administration is still playing catch-up.

Chinese companies have been steadily building their control over key mining and refining operations for decades, giving Beijing the ability to turn off supplies to the rest of the world at will.

Take just one example: copper, the world’s most important industrial metal, used in electrical wiring, circuit boards and plumbing. Analysts predict the world is headed for a supply crunch in the coming years – and China is far better placed to weather it.

The Middle Kingdom’s companies control around 16pc of global copper production compared to 6pc owned by American firms, according to data from Benchmark Mineral Intelligence, and there have been calls in China to boost domestic stockpiles.

“China has been doing this for a while, so this isn’t new for them,” says Albert Mackenzie, an analyst at Benchmark.

“The US may have looked at how they have built up their copper assets overseas, and is now trying to replicate that or not get left behind.

“There’s a big desire for more copper at the moment – you can point at AI, or to the energy transition, but actually it’s in basically everything we use because it’s such a good conductor.”

Minerals monopoly

While China has a grip on supply, its dominance of critical minerals is really about processing, with its share standing at over 50pc for a whole host of metals and exceeding 90pc for certain products.

This means that even though such metals may be mined in the US, Australia or elsewhere, they are still sent to China for refining.

Some 91pc of rare earth magnet metal refining and 94pc of global magnet production is concentrated in China, according to the International Energy Agency.

These are the magnets used in critical parts of the US military’s F-35 stealth fighter, as well as Tomahawk cruise missiles, navy destroyers and submarines, the Stinger portable missile and the M1 Abrams tank.

This leverage was brought into painful focus for Mr Trump last year, when he was forced to walk back his threats of trade tariffs after Beijing imposed export restrictions on heavy rare earths.

Ahead of his visit to China in April, the US president has now approved the creation of a $12bn critical mineral reserve, known as Project Vault, that will seek to guard US manufacturers against similar threats in future.

The initiative has been backed by captains of industry including the bosses of GE Vernova and Boeing.

“We don’t want to ever go through what we went through a year ago,” Mr Trump told reporters last week.

“Just as we have long had a strategic petroleum reserve and a stockpile of critical minerals for national defence, we’re now creating this reserve for American industry, so we don’t have any problems.”

Security and stability

The US administration has also been seeking to rally allies around an international network for developing critical mineral supplies outside of China.

Last week, vice-president JD Vance and Marco Rubio, the secretary of state, hosted a critical minerals summit in Washington aimed at drumming up support for the initiative.

In a speech, Mr Rubio pointed out that the Chinese grip over supplies posed the same danger to all of America’s allies and urged them to work together.

“No country in the world has every critical mineral that’s valuable,” he said. “So it will rely on international cooperation.

“Right now, you have 90-something per cent of some of these elements controlled by one nation, one place. And that’s just not healthy for the global economy and it’s certainly not healthy for security and stability.”

Along with the deals that have been announced, the White House has also vowed to back minerals producers with grants, loans and even “price floors” that will ensure they get paid a minimum amount for their minerals.

This is seen as vital because China’s producers receive large amounts of state support and can otherwise undercut competition from private Western firms.

Dominic Raab, the former Conservative deputy prime minister who now works with minerals investor Appian Capital Advisory, was in the room and says this change in mindset is key.

“There’s a recognition that it’s not a free market, it’s a broken market,” he says. “So they’re going to have to engage at a state level, but they also want to unlock private sector investment, and that’s important for de-risking those investments and unlocking more capital.”

Sophia Gaston, a geopolitics researcher at King’s College London, says the showdown with Beijing last year left Washington officials chastened by America’s “profound vulnerabilities on these supply chains”.

“China called the Trump administration’s bluff, and that forced America back to the negotiation table, and I think they felt extremely exposed by that, and wanted to move quickly to try and prevent that kind of situation happening again,” she says.

“There’s been a very intense flurry of activity, because Donald Trump is pencilled in for a visit to Beijing in April. So I think he wants to feel he’s going into that conversation with the maximum leverage.

“Until that critical minerals and rare earths diversification strategy starts to come into view, that’s going to be difficult – because there will always be a feeling that China can turn off the tap.”
