data.title: London’s listed landlords have been unloved for too long
data.description:
Questor, The Telegraph’s investing column, takes a weekly view of the markets – what is moving them, what lies ahead and how all of this could affect your portfolios and financial goals. 

In the wake of the global financial crisis, the big banks traded at a discount for what seemed like forever. The market had given up on them and the narrative was working strongly against them.

This, of course, did not last forever.

During 2021 and 2024, the gold mining industry became a prime target for takeovers thanks to bargain-basement prices, and many deals were struck at a hefty premium.

This, too, did not last forever.

Today, one of the London Stock Exchange’s least loved sectors has a narrative working strongly against it: hefty discounts, lowly valuations and plenty of takeover interest.

So great is the interest that an £8bn bid by the Swiss Zurich Insurance for Lloyd’s of London syndicate manager Beazley is being drowned out by a paltry £150m deal.

Even though the Zurich bid equates to a 63pc premium to Beazley’s undisturbed, pre-bid share price, this column – and more than a few UK equity fund managers and analysts besides, based on recent conversations – are paying more attention to the smaller deal.

This is the cash-and-stock bid from British Land for Life Science Reit. At around £150m, representing a proposed 21pc takeover premium, the putative transaction is surely not as eye-catching.

But this is just the latest in a string of bids for UK real estate companies, and it may well be time to give the nation’s stock market-listed landlords a little more love.

Property plunge

Over the last five years, only nine of the 38 industrial sectors that comprise the FTSE 350 index have fallen in value. Real estate investment trusts (Reits) and real estate investment and services are two of them, with respective falls of 19pc and 21pc over that span.

(The best performer is aerospace and defence, with a 539pc capital gain, before dividends, followed by banks with a 232pc advance.)

It is easy to explain the wretched showing of Reits.

From an operational point of view, bricks-and-mortar retailers face an ongoing onslaught from online rivals to the detriment of demand for high street sites, while the rise of hybrid working is a threat to demand for offices. Meanwhile, the overall economic outlook remains murky.

The steady increase in interest rates between 2021 and 2023 may have crimped demand, hurt net asset value (Nav) calculations and made Reits’ dividend yields look less attractive relative to cash or Gilts.

These issues are all well known.

Less well known is that the property trade press is starting to report shortages of prime London office space. Interest rates still seem to be on a downward trajectory and Reits as a group trade at a sizeable discount to their latest reported Navs.

The current average discount across the sector is some 23pc, but the range is wide, from around zero, at Primary Health Properties and Sirius Real Estate, to more than 40pc at Unite Group, Workspace and Town Centre Securities.

The range partly reflects different target markets between the Reits – which is also why they require careful research – but the gap between price and value is attracting predators.

Sometimes these are trade buyers, as was the case when Belgium’s Shurgard bought Lok’n’Store in 2024, others are financial buyers, such as when investment group Blackstone snapped up Warehouse Reit in 2025.

There have been 13 bids made for London-listed real estate stocks in the past three years. The average bid premium is 24pc, with the highest north of 40pc. The average discount to historic Nav per share represented by those offers is just under 12pc, a figure much closer to asset value than the current sector valuation.

Mind the gap

Experienced investors know that buying a stock in the hope of a bid is a mug’s game. The real point is to buy well-run, well-financed businesses that have a strong competitive position at a sensible price, and tuck them away for the long term so dividends can be farmed (and if possible reinvested). Any takeover is simply a bonus.

But when the takeover activity is reminiscent of that seen in the gold mining industry during 2021 and 2024, and the lowly valuations also sound like an echo of the big banks, it would be foolish not to pay attention.

Eventually canny management teams started to buy back bank stock.

We are seeing the takeovers but not, intriguingly, the buybacks in the Reit sector. Perhaps Saba’s arrival on the share register of both Life Science Reit and Workspace will prompt further action, of one kind or another.

data.title: Traffic lights should turn green for cyclists, says Lime bike boss
data.description:
Traffic lights should automatically turn green for cyclists, according to the boss of Lime bikes.

Hal Stevenson, policy director for Lime, claimed it could reduce the number of cyclists ignoring red lights.

Transport for London (TfL) last month unveiled plans to give buses priority at all 3,500 traffic lights they pass in London by 2030.

Mr Stevenson called for the measure to be extended to bikes, saying: “Cyclists shouldn’t be left on red.”

Critics described the suggestion as “farcical”.

Mr Stevenson stated that TfL had extended London’s cycling network from about 90 km (56 miles) in 2016 to 430 km (267 miles) last year, with traffic light changes “the natural next step”.

“Rethinking how traffic lights are timed could be a natural next step in building on that success,” he wrote in an article for MyLondon. “Other leading cycling cities like Copenhagen and Amsterdam show what’s possible.

“Green-wave technology, which rewards cyclists travelling at a safe, consistent speed with a sequence of green lights, has improved safety and rider behaviour while reducing journey times at relatively low cost. It could also help address red-light running.”

He said traffic lights were currently timed “around motor traffic speeds”, which “led to frequent stopping for people on bikes”.

“Cycling now accounts for up to one in five trips in central London, with cycle traffic at peak times exceeding cars,” he added.

“Despite this, signal timings remain set around motor traffic speeds, which can lead to frequent stopping and less smooth journeys for people on bikes.”

Steve McNamara, general secretary of the Licensed Taxi Drivers Association, said the policy would be “farcical” because London is “incredibly congested” with traffic “slower than it’s ever been”.

He claimed people who hire bikes are “incentivised” to take “dangerous risks” such as ignoring red lights because the companies’ time-based pricing means it is cheaper if journeys are completed more quickly.

Mr McNamara said he attended road safety meetings alongside members of the “white, middle-class cycling lobby” whose “whole obsession is removing vehicle traffic from the roads”.

He added: “Has it not occurred to them that some people either can’t or don’t want to cycle?

“It doesn’t mean there’s anything wrong with them.”

A recent report by location technology company TomTom found London was the world’s slowest capital city to drive in last year.

It took an average of three minutes and 38 seconds to drive one kilometre (0.6 miles) in the centre of London last year, according to the analysis.

This was partly blamed on widespread 20mph speed limits.

Reducing speed limits from 30mph to 20mph has been one of Sir Sadiq Khan’s key transport policies as part of his ambition to reduce road casualties.

A spokesman for the mayor said journey times on the TfL road network “remain at similar levels to those in 2019” and “studies have shown 20mph limits and zones may improve traffic flow and cut traffic jams”.

A TfL spokesman said: “We already take cyclists, as well as pedestrians and other traffic, into account when we review the timings of around 1,200 signals each year.

“We’re also working to transform the technology behind London’s traffic signals, moving towards an intelligent adaptive traffic signal system that optimises the amount of green time for everyone.”

data.title: Murdoch to take over printing of The Guardian
data.description:
Rupert Murdoch will take over printing The Guardian after the newspaper’s current printer closes down most of its production sites.

The newspaper is currently printed by Reach, which owns titles including the Mirror and Express. However, Reach this week outlined plans to shutter two of its three remaining print sites in an effort to cut costs.

The overhaul means that the majority of Guardian newspapers will now be printed by Newsprinters, a joint venture between Mr Murdoch’s News UK and Daily Mail publisher DMGT, at its site in Broxbourne, Hertfordshire.

A smaller number of copies that are currently printed at the Reach site in Glasgow will be transferred to Scottish publisher DC Thomson.

The transfer, which is due to be completed by July under a 10-year outsourcing agreement, puts The Guardian’s print operations under the control of one of its most deep-seated ideological opponents.

The enmity between the two publishers dates back to The Guardian’s key role in uncovering phone hacking at Mr Murdoch’s News of the World.

The Guardian’s reporting, led by Nick Davies, exposed the widespread interception of voicemails and police bribery by journalists at the tabloid.

The fallout led to the closure of the News of the World in 2011 as well as the high-profile Leveson inquiry into press ethics.

Mr Murdoch’s empire has paid out more than £1bn to settle claims and legal expenses relating to phone hacking.

The saga was dramatised last year in The Hack, an ITV series starring David Tennant and Toby Jones.

The Guardian has also attacked Mr Murdoch and his grip on the British media in its own pages. News UK owns The Sun and The Times as well as broadcasters including Virgin Radio and Talk.

Opinion columns in the Left-leaning newspaper have previously accused the nonagenarian media mogul of “brainwashing” Britain and leaving a “toxic legacy” that gave rise to Donald Trump and the alt-Right.

The closure of Reach’s print sites comes as the news industry adapts to a sharp decline in newspaper circulation as audiences shift online. This has been compounded by the surging cost of newsprint and other inflationary pressures.

Piers North, the chief executive of Reach, said in a message to staff earlier this week: “Given the current customer demand for printed products across the UK and trajectory for the future, I believe now is the right time to consolidate our printing work, to continue to serve our customers and deliver on our strategy.”

In 2024, News UK and DMGT were granted approval by competition regulators to merge their printing operations into a single company.

At the time of the deal, the publishers said the combined company would print on average over three million newspapers each night and almost 22 million per week. The Telegraph is also printed under contract by Newsprinters.

A source close to The Guardian stressed that its contract remained with Reach despite the deal to outsource to Newsprinters.

The Guardian pursued a costly print overhaul in 2005 when it switched to the “Berliner” format. It spent £80m to build specially commissioned new printing presses but later ditched the format and went for tabloid sizing.

data.title: Warren Buffett’s final investment: The New York Times
data.description:
Warren Buffett has used his last major bet before retirement to invest $352m (£260m) in The New York Times.

The legendary investor’s Berkshire Hathaway vehicle bought the stake in the US newspaper in the last three months of 2025, before Mr Buffett stepped down as chief executive in January.

At the same time, Mr Buffett has trimmed his stake in Apple – his largest holding – and sold three quarters of his Amazon investment, six years after first backing the online retailer.

Mr Buffett, 95, ended six decades at the top of Berkshire Hathaway by making way for Greg Abel, the company’s vice chairman, earlier this year.

The news of Berkshire buying 5.1 million shares in The New York Times sent its stock climbing by 3.5pc to an all-time high on Tuesday night.

Mr Buffett was a decades-long investor in The Washington Post until Jeff Bezos purchased the paper in 2013, and owned a string of local papers until 2020 when he sold off the holdings, having said many titles were “toast”.

But major titles have enjoyed a renaissance in recent years amid the shift to digital publishing.

Shares in the New York Times have climbed by 500pc in the last decade, with the organisation currently valued at around $12bn, after investing in subscriptions, games, cooking and sports.

Mr Buffett, dubbed the “Sage of Omaha” for his prescient investments, holds stakes in dozens of companies through his Berkshire Hathaway conglomerate.

The newspaper stake was its only new investment, although Berkshire also increased its stake in Chevron before Donald Trump’s seizure of Nicolás Maduro’s Venezuela sent oil company shares soaring.

Besides selling Amazon and trimming Apple, Berkshire also cut its exposure to Bank of America. The company has spent years gradually selling its Apple shares, which remain its biggest investment.

Berkshire Hathaway, based in Omaha, Nebraska, was acquired by Buffett in 1965 and has since delivered extraordinary returns for investors.

It has averaged an average annual return of 19.8pc since then, compared to the S&P 500’s 10.2pc.

Mr Buffett owns 13.7pc of the company, giving him a net worth of $150bn, making him the world’s 10th-richest person.

While investors had long speculated about when he would retire, he shocked them last May with the news that he was retiring, two years after the death of his long-time right-hand man, Charlie Munger.

Mr Buffett remains Berkshire Hathaway’s chairman.

data.title: Bring back the great British TV drama of whisky-swilling businessmen
data.description:
The way we view television has changed enormously in the 73 years since Queen Elizabeth II’s coronation. We’ve gone from flickering black-and-white boxes that took a good 30 seconds to warm up and served up just one channel, to widescreen OLED smart devices that stream thousands of choices of “content” (though, ironically, they still often take a good 30 seconds to start up).

But the actual shows? Although they look very different, the genres of TV drama have stayed remarkably steady over all that time. Flick through the TV listings from any year over the decades, and you will find crime shows, medical shows, soaps, sci-fi, costume drama, historical series, and so on. There’s not all that great a gap between Emergency Ward 10 and Casualty. There’s only one kind of drama series that you will find in a 1966 edition of TV Times that you won’t see while scrolling through your EPG in 2026 (excluding westerns, which were obviously never a British concern anyway). I’m talking about business TV.

There used to be many of these series: The Brothers, The Power Game, The Plane Makers, Mogul, The Troubleshooters. These were gripping stories of men (and it was overwhelmingly men) striding into each others’ offices, pouring whiskies, sparking up cigars, and obsessing over boards, stocks and shares, new products and processes. Women did feature, but until the Eighties they were the wives and secretaries that orbited like satellites around the boardrooms and factory floors where the main events played out.

Now, I will hear you object that boardroom business drama survives – Succession or Industry, for example. The big difference between these modern shows and their forebears is that they aren’t really concerned with the actual business of the business, except where it directly affects the drama between the characters.

Succession didn’t have a storyline about the design of a product – you can’t imagine scenes with Shiv or Roman unfurling plans across a big teak table, or lengthy discussions about the pros and cons of the fuel injection nozzle or whatever. No, Succession is more the inheritor of the Dallas model, the high-glamour soap in which the actual work in hand is merely the convenient backdrop.

The true business TV drama of old portrayed the actual business as dramatic in itself. The heyday of the form was 1963’s The Plane Makers, the story of an aviation design firm which after three series reinvented itself in 1965 as The Power Game, shifting its anti-hero John Wilder (Patrick Wymark) to a merchant bank. The tensions of manufacture, negotiations and industrial relations were depicted in detail that seems baffling today.

There is a sense of great public interest in business in other shows of the time. Early Honor Blackman episodes of The Avengers feature Steed and Mrs Gale investigating chicanery at works making pottery and optical lenses, and trying to expose export licences for counterfeit packaging. It’s all very unusual to 21st-century eyes.

The Wymark model – the tycoon stomping about, all blood and thunder, in his executive suite – became such an established part of TV that Fry and Laurie would spoof it in their John and Peter sketches: “Damn it to damnation, triple blast, John, I want to know what the hell is going on!”

The sometimes strained and phoney scene-setting dialogue of the genre – as we pick up the characters before the meat of the scene – was also brilliantly lampooned by Victoria Wood, who opens a visit to Acorn Antiques with Miss Babs pointing at blank sheets of paper and telling Miss Berta, “Yes, that’s much better – if we deliver these antiques on Friday morning, we can take delivery of these antiques in the afternoon.”

By the Seventies, the genre had become so established that it started generating oddities of its own. Hine, from 1971, is a fascinatingly weird one-off, created by Power Game writer Wilfred Greatorex (what a fantastic “Dammit, Marjorie!” name that is).

Hine starred the personable Barrie Ingham as the titular hero, who is a freelance international arms dealer. Viewers were baffled – were we really meant to be rooting for this charming chap in his attempts to sell British weapons to dictatorships in the Middle East? – and the show flopped. It stands now as a fascinatingly amoral glimpse into a hidden world.

The episode “The Old School Noose” by Arden Winch (another great moniker and nothing to do with the workwear firm of the same name), in which Hine is caught in an elaborate web involving his childhood best friend who is now a Labour MP, is one of those exquisite pieces of dramatic writing and acting that occasionally startles you in a vintage “hack-for-hire” series.

So what became of the “pure” business drama? The last British example I can think of that still retained elements of it was Eighties super-soap Howards’ Way, the BBC’s answer to Dynasty. Much of the action in its initial stages revolved around the design and production of a new racing boat, and the dilemma between using hand-crafted or machine-made mouldings. Try pitching that to Netflix. But fairly soon this faded into the background, swallowed up by the ins and outs of Relton Marine, Leisurecruise, the House of Howard, and which character owned what percentage of them.

In fact, in the Nineties, business started infecting our less glam soaps. When I worked as a writer on Coronation Street, Fiona Middleton – aged 21! – owned the salon, while Brookside’s teenage entrepreneur Jacqui Dixon had fingers in investment pies everywhere, and in EastEnders Sharon Watts became Walford’s answer to Howard Hughes. These may have been intended as inspiring messages of female empowerment, but they were both highly unlikely and difficult for viewers to identify with.

The simple answer to the mystery of business TV’s decline is that Britain stopped manufacturing things. There are currently politicians on all sides mooting the return of long-defunct industries to our land. In the unlikely event that they succeed, perhaps once again viewers will see people slamming down tumblers of Scotch and bellowing at their secretaries, damn it!

data.title: Labour’s property market plot could hammer your house price
data.description:
When Harold Wilson launched Labour’s housebuilding blitz in 1964, he hoped to tap into Britain’s wartime spirit of two decades earlier to unleash a British building renaissance.

“Just as in the war, the idea of a common purpose had a dynamising effect, so possibly the launching of a great housing plan could have a similar effect today,” he told Labour members.

Wilson’s Labour government went on to phenomenal success, building over 400,000 homes a year by 1968 – a UK record which is still to be surpassed.

Almost 60 years on and Labour’s latest crop of ministers are wrestling with a similar housing crisis.

They have pledged to build 300,000 homes a year while in power.

But instead of Wilson’s soaring rhetoric, ministers are pushing a more puzzling message to galvanise the public: to help more young people get on the property ladder, we are going to hammer the price of your house to help make things fairer.

Last week, Matthew Pennycook, the housing minister, admitted that house prices will fall if Labour meets its target to build 1.5 million homes this parliament.

“Our analysis, in broad terms, estimates that a 1pc increase in housing stock should … decrease prices by 2pc,” Pennycook told a Commons committee on Tuesday.

Pennycook, a 43-year-old former think tank academic from west London, said more housebuilding would lead to a “sustained levelling-off of house price growth, and then a gradual easing of it over time”.

It is an aim that may resonate with young people locked out of the property market.

But for swathes of British homeowners, it has sparked fears that Labour is now coming for their property nest eggs.

Property is an obsession in this country because it comprises 40pc of household wealth in the UK – with much of that going towards funding retirement.

“People who paid off their mortgages and have got an awful lot of equity tied up in their housing are protective of the price of their houses,” says Paul Cheshire, a professor at the London School of Economics.

“There is a tension between what you might call the housing ‘haves’ and the housing ‘have-nots’. The balance is moving towards the housing have-nots.”

In a bid to calm fears, Steve Reed, the Housing Secretary, was rushed out after Pennycook’s appearance to dispel the idea that Labour wanted to hurt house prices.

“We don’t want house prices to come down where people own their own home,” he said.

“We need to build more homes so it’s more affordable for young people in particular to get that first foot on the housing ladder.”

Yet the alarming messaging around the plan has prompted further scrutiny on Labour’s building policy.

The party came to power in 2024 promising a housing revolution.

Guided by Angela Rayner, the former deputy prime minister, it said 1.5 million homes would be built by 2030 – about 300,000 a year.

Today, Labour claims it has started 310,000 homes since it came into power, equalling an average monthly rate of 17,200.

This leaves 1.2 million to finish in the remaining 42 months until July 2029 – meaning they need to build 28,000 per month.

Yet, according to Tom Bill, head of UK residential research at Knight Frank, attempts to bring down house prices by increasing supply “seems like a simple economic truth but [it] fundamentally misunderstands how the sector operates”.

“Housebuilders deliver homes at a rate and price that is sustainable for their business, not because it’s written down in a political manifesto,” he says.

“The whole development process needs to be smoother and affordability is a genuine concern in some areas, but this latest intervention is like asking a car manufacturer to speed up its production line in order to sell cheaper hatchbacks,” Bill adds.

Britain has faced a severe shortage of housing for decades. Since 2000, house prices have risen by nearly 250pc, locking many out of the market and forcing them to rent or live under their parents’ roofs.

For Pennycook, much of the blame for this is on the housebuilders and the property industry.

“People in London that bought land at the top of the market for too high a price and are now sitting on it. And there will probably need to be a market adjustment in London,” he told the Financial Times last month.

Tim Leunig, a former adviser to Rishi Sunak, called Pennycook’s objective a “sensible aspiration” but doubted Labour could make it stick.

“If prices fell by 1pc or 2pc a year for a while that would be great,” he said. “Add in inflation … and real wage growth … and you are looking at a 5pc improvement in affordability a year, or close to 25pc in a parliament.

“That would be transformational for a lot of people. Sadly [Labour’s] policies are unlikely to deliver that.”

Builders have also disputed Pennycook’s diagnosis of the problem, saying his claims are “bonkers”.

Graham Prothero, chief executive of housebuilder MJ Gleeson, says: “There appears to be a bit of an ideology that says, if developers didn’t ask for such high profits, then houses would be cheap and everything would be wonderful. It cannot be that simple.”

He adds that he was “very disappointed” at an implication that prices are high because developers sit on land so that it can gain more value over time.

“If my business is to make money out of selling houses, why on earth would I hold them back on the basis that I might make speculative profit out of a green field?” says Prothero.

“It is absolutely bonkers. The moment I buy that site, it’s costing me money. What I want to do is get it through planning and sell the houses, then I can start to close off my risk and actually hopefully sell [at] a profit.”

Politically, the mixed signals coming from the housing department also underscore an ideological divide between the different factions of the Labour Party.

With the likes of Andy Burnham and Ed Miliband applying more pressure on Sir Keir to lean Left after swerving last week’s leadership challenge, Pennycook’s stance is likely to be interpreted as a way for Sir Keir to score some political points.

Many voters have children who may be stuck at home and need help with funding their deposits for a place of their own.

But it could alienate the share of Labour voters who own properties and have swung towards the party away from the Conservatives in recent years.

In the General Election, Labour secured a 17-point lead among mortgage holders and reduced the Tories’ advantage with outright homeowners from 35 points to just 10 points, an Ipsos poll found.

Labour has largely pinned its housebuilding ambitions on bringing in planning reforms to ease the regulatory burden on property developers.

London, which underpins much of Labour’s housebuilding target, remains a fundamental concern for many in the industry, given it remains hugely expensive relative to people’s incomes.

Housebuilding has slumped by 84pc over the past decade, according to Molior, with construction starting on just 5,547 private homes in 2025.

“In London, we are nowhere near building enough to keep pace with half of what demand is, let alone outpace demand and bring prices down,” says Sam Dumitriu, the head of policy at the think tank Britain Remade.

‘Bad deal’ for builders

Building thousands of new homes every year also has implications if they are marketed incorrectly.

Dumitriu points out that putting every single home on a newly built estate on the market at once would lead to “quite a lot of vacancy” and create potential for negative price impacts.

“You might say that it’s a good deal for consumers, a bad deal for the builder, but fundamentally the builder has to factor that in when they build it,” he says.

Labour and Pennycook are due to set out a new housing strategy in the coming months.

With Labour’s last building blitz in the 1960s a distant memory, Sir Keir and his colleagues will need to find their own wartime spirit to achieve their aims.

A spokesman at the Ministry of Housing, Communities and Local Government said: “We are fixing the housing crisis we inherited by accelerating reforms to get Britain building and restore the dream of home ownership.”

The spokesman added: “Major planning changes are already under way and we’re investing £39bn in social and affordable housing.

“Our landmark plan for new towns across the country will also create more attractive, well-designed places where people want to live.”

data.title: British Gas owner seeks to extend nuclear plants’ lifespans into 2030s
data.description:
British Gas owner Centrica is planning to keep its oldest nuclear plants running into the 2030s to help strengthen the UK’s energy supplies.

The FTSE 100 giant said on Thursday that it supported plans to extend the lifespan of its fleet of 1980s power stations and was “increasingly confident” that such a move would be granted.

The company also revealed that profits almost halved from £1.55bn to £814m last year as gas and power prices tumbled and bosses bet on a major investment in the future Sizewell C nuclear plant. It has pledged to commit up to £600m on the project by the end of 2028.

Shares in Centrica plunged by more than 9pc following the update, wiping more than £800m off its market value.

Along with France’s EDF, the energy giant is a shareholder in all five of Britain’s remaining nuclear sites.

Of these, four advanced gas-cooled reactor (AGR) sites are currently scheduled to shut between 2028 and 2030 – Hartlepool, Heysham One, Heysham Two and Torness – while the fifth plant, Sizewell B, is expected to be extended into the 2050s.

However, the Government warned last month that the AGR closures risked leaving “a dangerous gap in Britain’s low-carbon energy supply” because they would happen before new plants came online. It said it supported safely extending them if possible.

Chris O’Shea, the chief executive of Centrica, said on Thursday that Hartlepool and Heysham One were currently due to shut in 2028 while Torness and Heysham Two were due to shut by 2030.

However, he added: “We are increasingly confident that we will see lifetime extensions so that all of those plants can go into the early 2030s.

“Probably you will see a number of one or two-year extensions there.”

Extensions for Hartlepool and Heysham One could be announced later this year, he said.

EDF confirmed last month it had “an ambition to generate low carbon electricity from all four generating AGR stations for as long as it is safe and commercially viable to do so”.

The French company added: “Now the stations are in the latter stages of their generating lives, further extensions will not be for long.

“We will keep their lifetimes under review to assess whether further life extensions can be achieved.”

EDF said their lifespans will ultimately depend on safety inspections of their graphite cores.

Over time, the cores develop cracks that can prevent the reactor’s control rods from being operated properly. EDF has previously stressed that safety rules mean it would have to decommission the plant long before enough of the rods became inoperable.

Any extension would have to be supported by the Office for Nuclear Regulation, an independent quango.

The plant closures would mean Britain’s nuclear power output would tumble to between just three to four gigawatts by 2030, down from six gigawatts today and a peak of 12.7 gigawatts in the mid-1990s.

Two new power plants, Hinkley Point C in Somerset and Sizewell C in Suffolk, are under construction but won’t come online until the early 2030s and late 2030s, respectively.

Sir Keir Starmer said earlier this year that the Government wanted to “avoid a ‘nuclear gap’ by enabling existing stations to continue running where demonstrably safe, such as through the potential 20-year lifetime extension of Sizewell B and by getting Hinkley Point C and Sizewell C online as soon as possible”.

Centrica blamed its drop in profits on “challenging” market conditions but pointed to “bold investments” in projects such as Sizewell C that would bring future revenues.

European gas prices peaked at about €56 (£49) per megawatt hour in 2025 before tumbling by about 50pc through the rest of the year.

Centrica said the annual loss also included a reappraisal of energy trading contracts that cost £700m, a £500m write-down on “late-life” gas fields and costs related to investment in its nuclear portfolio.

The company said it was increasing the dividend by 22pc but was pausing its share buyback programme.

data.title: NHS to pay £3bn more for drugs to escape Trump tariffs
data.description:
The NHS has agreed to pay 25pc more for new medicines to escape Donald Trump’s sweeping new tariffs on drugs.

The US president unveiled a new pharmaceutical trade deal with Britain on Monday, striking an agreement to impose zero import tariffs on medical products from the UK.

It will see British medicines carved out from sweeping new levies, offering a major boost for one of Britain’s biggest exports to America. In 2024, the UK exported around £6.6bn in pharmaceutical and medical products to the US.

The agreement includes a pledge for the NHS to increase the net price it pays for new medicines by 25pc, which will add around £3bn to the taxpayer’s £12bn annual medical spending bill. This will be phased in.

It follows weeks of uncertainty over the future of the industry. In August, the US president threatened to impose tariffs of up to 250pc on all pharmaceutical imports – including the UK.

In September, he said there would be 100pc tariffs on “any branded or patented Pharmaceutical Product” coming into the US from October 1. At the time, he said companies could get an exemption if they had started building factories in the US.

While the White House has agreed to a spate of country-specific trade deals over the past year, pharma tariffs are expected to fall outside the scope of these agreements.

Under the new agreement, the NHS has also committed to slashing rebates that drug companies have to pay on sales of newer medicines to the health service.

The so-called Voluntary Scheme for Branded Medicines Pricing, Access and Growth (VPAG) is designed to cap the overall NHS drug bill but pharmaceutical companies have complained it has failed to keep pace with inflation.

Additionally, the deal means the UK will cut the rebate rate from 22.9pc to 15pc in 2026.

It is also understood that the Government will increase the threshold that determines which treatments are available on the NHS, boosting the spending cap on certain medicines and paving the way for more cutting-edge treatments to be introduced.

Drug giants have long complained over the decades-old formula used to determine whether medicines are deemed value for money, claiming this does not allow them to get the best new drugs into the NHS.

Jamieson Greer, the US trade representative, said: “For too long, American patients have been forced to subsidise prescription drugs and biologics in other developed countries by paying a significant premium for the same products in ours.

“Today, the United States and the United Kingdom announce this negotiated outcome pricing for innovative pharmaceuticals, which will help drive investment and innovation in both countries.”

He added: “The Trump Administration is reviewing the pharmaceutical pricing practices of many other US trading partners and hopes that they will follow suit with constructive negotiations.”

Howard Lutnick, the US commerce secretary, said the deal would bring new jobs to America.

Mr Lutnick said: “This deal doesn’t just deepen our economic partnership with the United Kingdom – it ensures that the breakthroughs of tomorrow will be built, tested and produced on American soil.”

The White House has previously made clear its frustration over the lack of spending on innovative medicines in Britain. Currently, the UK spends just 0.28pc of its GDP on new medicines, roughly a third of what America does.

Britain previously pledged to review the health service’s drug pricing to take into account “the concerns of the president” under the UK-US trade deal struck in May.

Mr Trump has called for an end to overseas countries “freeloading” on American innovation, singling out Britain for a place where medicine prices are unfairly low compared to the US.

The US ambassador last month said Britain needed to change how it priced medicines or risk more global drug companies scrapping investments in the UK.

Merck, one of America’s largest drug companies, this year ditched plans to open a £1bn research centre in London, while AstraZeneca has paused £200m spending on its Cambridge research facility.

Liz Kendall, the Science and Technology Secretary, said the deal would “enable and incentivise life sciences companies to continue to invest and innovate right here in the UK”.

Chris Boerner, chief executive of Bristol Myers Squibb, said the trade agreement meant the pharmaceutical company will invest “upwards of $500m (£378m)” over the next five years.

Mr Boerner said: “This investment will come across multiple areas of the business including research, development and manufacturing. This agreement is a sign of progress and one that creates an environment conducive to our continued presence in the UK.”

data.title: Debt-laden Very Group secures £150m breathing space for sale
data.description:
The lender that seized control of Very from the Barclay family has provided the online retailer with £150m in financial support while it attempts to engineer a sale.

US investment giant Carlyle has pumped new money into Very as part of a support package to ease financial pressures on the debt-laden business.

As well as providing a cash injection, Carlyle has also converted some of its debt into equity to help reduce pressure on the retailer from interest payments.

The Telegraph understands that Carlyle’s total package is £150m.

The support is part of a broader refinancing, which has been carried out as Carlyle tries to sell the company for £2bn.

Bankers at Barclays and JPMorgan have been tasked with selling the business after Carlyle took control of Very in November 2025, ending the Barclay family’s involvement in the retailer after two decades.

Before that, Carlyle had been the retailer’s main corporate lender.

International Media Investments (IMI), the Abu Dhabi media vehicle that was involved in a failed effort to acquire The Telegraph from the Barclay family, also became a major lender to Very in that complex transaction.

Both Carlyle and IMI helped to keep the Liverpool-based business afloat as the Barclays’ business empire unravelled.

Carlyle is now seeking to put the company, which sells a wide range of clothes, toys and electrical goods, on a more stable footing ahead of a potential sale.

In addition to its own £150m support package, which is aimed at reducing debts, Very is also understood to have secured more breathing space from other external lenders. Bosses are expected to announce details of the financing on Monday.

The business has extended a £150m credit facility for another three years until February 2030, while also prolonging a £1.8bn “securitisation facility” until February 2029.

Otherwise known as consumer credit, this £1.8bn pot provided by a consortium of banks was set to expire next year.

Very has also secured better terms on some of its borrowings, which were a total of £2.3bn last year, according to company filings. That is compared with revenues of £2bn from 4.4 million customers.

Robbie Feather, chief executive of Very, hailed the company’s strong trading performance over Christmas when sales grew 1.9pc in the six weeks to Dec 27.

The latest refinancing follows a prolonged period of uncertainty for Very, which was caught up in the crisis engulfing the Barclays.

The family had launched an aborted attempt to sell the business in early 2025 before Carlyle seized control in November.

The change in ownership led to Very posting losses of £500m in 2025 after it was forced to write off a major loan to its former owners. This brought an end to the family’s involvement in the business.

Very, which was once viewed as the jewel in the crown of the Barclays’ empire, was formed 20 years ago through the merger of Littlewoods and Shop Direct, overseen by Sir Frederick Barclay and the late Sir David Barclay.

Aidan Barclay, the eldest of Sir David’s four sons, chaired the business until May 2024, when he was replaced by Nadhim Zahawi.

As well as losing control of Very, the Barclays have also lost a string of other prized assets in recent years, including The Telegraph.

Very declined to comment.
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: British AI trailblazer raising $1bn for three-month-old start-up
data.description:
A British artificial intelligence (AI) researcher is in talks to raise $1bn (£740m) for his three-month-old start-up in what would be one of the biggest ever venture funding rounds in Europe.

Prof David Silver, a former scientist at Google’s DeepMind lab, is close to raising hundreds of millions of pounds for his new business, Ineffable Intelligence, from investors including Silicon Valley fund Sequoia.

The deal would be the largest “seed” investment – a start-up’s first equity funding round – of any British company on record. It is expected to value Ineffable at $4bn, the Financial Times reported.

Prof Silver, a research scientist at University College London, joined DeepMind shortly after it was launched in 2010 and is a friend of Sir Demis Hassabis, its founder and chief executive.

He led some of its work on AlphaGo, an AI programme that made history in 2016 by beating Lee Se-dol, the top player of the ancient Chinese strategy game, at Go.

The game was seen as a particularly difficult challenge for AI to master, given its complexity and vast numbers of potential moves and strategies.

Prof Silver, 49, was also one of the DeepMind experts who helped to develop Google’s Gemini AI tool, which is a rival to OpenAI’s ChatGPT.

AI experts have been hot property amongst technology investors chasing the next breakthrough after the launch of ChatGPT in 2022. A handful of AI researchers who helped to set up OpenAI or other labs have raised billions of dollars to develop new products.

Mira Murati, a former OpenAI executive, raised $2bn in 2025 just months after launching her start-up, Thinking Machines.

Ilya Sutskever, OpenAI’s former technology chief, has raised $3bn for a new AI lab since leaving the ChatGPT developer in 2024.

The funding round for Ineffable Intelligence would make Prof Silver’s start-up one of the best-funded British start-ups in the AI business. The company was founded on Nov 19 last year, according to Companies House filings, making it just three months old.

Prof Silver was one of the pioneers of so-called “reinforcement learning”, which uses feedback from human trainers or simulations to improve AI bots.

According to the Financial Times, Microsoft, Nvidia and Google have also held talks to invest in Prof Silver’s business.

In a research note published by Prof Silver last year, he predicted that AI tools would soon begin to learn from their own “experience”, which could “unlock unprecedented capabilities”.

He added that this method of learning would help to “eclipse the scale and quality of human-generated data”, as AI labs had struggled to obtain enough high-quality information to improve their bots.

Separately on Wednesday, Saudi Arabia’s state-owned AI lab confirmed it had poured $3bn into Elon Musk’s xAI, securing the Middle Eastern kingdom a major stake in the business.

Humain, an AI business controlled by the Saudi state’s Public Investment Fund, invested in Mr Musk’s AI business – which develops the controversial Grok bot – shortly before xAI merged with his space company.

That merger valued xAI at $250bn and SpaceX at $1tn, bringing together two arms of Mr Musk’s business empire. The combination comes before a widely expected initial public offering as soon as this summer.

It is the first major investment that the Saudi state has made in Mr Musk’s space business.

Ineffable Intelligence and Sequoia were contacted for comment.

data.title: The British-designed microchip that is powering Putin’s deadly missiles
data.description:
Yana Khudyo, 15, woke to find herself trapped and buried beneath the rubble of her home.

Two of her ribs and one leg had been shattered by an explosion.

It took rescuers 40 minutes to pull her from the wreckage after Igor, her 24-year-old brother, heard her calling beneath the debris.

Viktoria and Oleg, their parents, and Danylo, their 17-year-old brother, were already dead. It took rescuers another 18 hours to find their bodies, and for Igor to identify them.

What ripped the Khudyo family apart on April 24, 2025, was a KN-23 ballistic missile. And, at the heart of that tragedy, helping power the missile, there may have been a British microchip.

Fired by Russia, it struck a residential block in Kyiv’s Sviatoshynskyi district, murdering twelve Ukrainians as they slept.

After the attack, Volodymyr Zelensky, the Ukrainian president, said the weapon responsible for orphaning Yana contained 116 Western-made components.

Little additional detail was given.

The type of North Korean missile used in the strike has been found to contain British-made converters, according to Ukrainian investigators. 

It is one of thousands of Russian weapons that use Western – including British – dual-use military-grade components. Dual-use devices have both military and civilian use, meaning they can be used as easily in a computer as in a missile.

When Igor arrived at the site of the devastation, about 20 minutes after the strike, he saw his childhood home in smithereens.  

“I started desperately calling out to my mother, my father, to Daniel and to Yana,” he said from his apartment just outside Kyiv. 

“The mobile phones of the dead were ringing, but no one was picking up,” he continued as we sat in darkness caused by Russian air strikes.

“I tried to call Yana and when I heard her pick up the phone I knew she was alive. But there was no sound.

“She sent me a text which said ‘I am under rubble, I am dying’ and her rough location.

“I started frantically searching, despite Shahed drones circling overhead, until finally I heard her calling out to me from beneath the destruction.”

Yana had been thrown from her bed and trapped under a cupboard. 

She was operated on twice. “I found out my parents had died on Instagram while I was in the hospital,” she said.

“The psychologist was meant to tell me, but it was all over social media by the time I woke up from my surgeries.”

The impact of this experience on her is clear.

“I want to join the military [when I am 18],” she said proudly.

“This is the only future I see for myself because I want to be part of the people fighting for their land and their parents. I want to become a hero for Ukraine.”

With 16,000 international trading restrictions in place in Russia, how could British-made parts have found their way into Russia’s deadly arsenal, and why are sanctions not working?

The Telegraph and the Committee for Freedom in Hong Kong Foundation (CFHK), a human rights watchdog, are exposing how middlemen in Hong Kong have shipped thousands of dual-use components worth millions of dollars from Western Europe to Russia.

Campaigners want the Government to open an urgent “investigation” into how British parts reportedly ended up in weapons used against Ukraine, and to “close any loopholes” around pre-existing laws.

On Sept 7, 2024, a North Korean KN-23/24 ballistic missile with the serial number 312518759 was shot down in the villages of Myrne and Bilyky, Poltava Oblast, Ukraine.

It was one of 60 KN-23 or Hwasong 11A missiles supplied to Putin by Kim Jong-un and launched that year.

Buried among the rubble and shrapnel, investigators said they found two products labeled as coming from the British company XP Power: a DC/DC converter and a power converter.

Converters transfer direct currents from one voltage level to another and are commonly used to stabilise, boost, or lower a voltage within a machine.

XP Power, which has headquarters in Reading, Berks, said converters allegedly found in Russian weapons were either counterfeit or “sold before the invasion”. The firm has not provided any additional detail or evidence to support these assertations.

Both chips recovered in the rubble were allegedly manufactured in 2023, a year after the Kremlin’s invasion of Ukraine, and after XP Power said it had ceased trading with Russia.  

Russian customs data from 2022-2024, obtained by CFHK and reviewed by The Telegraph, found that 799 XP Power-labelled cargoes valued at around $2.5m (£1.8m) had been imported by Russia. It is not clear how XP Power-labelled devices ended up in North Korea. 

It is unknown where exactly the missiles containing the XP Power-branded converters were made in North Korea. The country expanded its missile production capactity in 2024. The Ryongsong Machine Complex in the Hamhung region is a key factory where these weapons are built.

The CFHK figures, which come from tracking the sale and shipments of high-risk goods to sanctioned countries, suggest almost half were sent via Hong Kong to electronic supply companies Woeroon Electronic Sourcing Limited, SSP LTS and Imaxchip. None of these companies responded to requests for comment.

It is not clear if these companies were seeking to breach sanctions, or if they were aware these shipments might be used by the Russian military.

XP Power disputed CFHK's findings, stating that “all orders from customers and shipments to customers are thoroughly checked for sanctioned countries and companies”.

The company said it had “never traded” directly with Woeroon, SSP LTS, Imaxchip or any of the companies named in the shipment data.

There is no suggestion that XP had any knowledge of where its components would end up, and no evidence the company was complicit in sending parts to Ukraine.

But it is common for middleman businesses committing sanctions evasion to pass dual-use stock from company to company.

“Naturally, we have less visibility of sales that might pass through a number of hands in the supply chain,” a spokesman for XP Power accepted.

She added: “We have stringent export controls in place and take our responsibilities under the various sanction regimes very seriously – after the invasion in February 2022, we immediately stopped all shipments to Russia.  

“We take our obligations in this area very seriously and we will continue to work with others in the supply chain to ensure their controls are as tight as ours.

“All of our sales contracts now include clauses to specifically prohibit onward sale or re-exporting to Russia.”

It is not just the KN-23 missile that has been found to contain British-made hardware.

British components have also been found in deadly Shahed drones, First-Person View drones, guided missiles and scores of other weapons launched by the Russian military into Ukraine.

In total, Ukrainian investigators have found 41 British-made components on the battlefield since the start of the war in 2022.  

On Dec 1 last year, the Ukrainian military downed a newly modified Geran-2 or Shahed attack drone carrying an R-60 infrared guided AAM (Air-to-Air Missile) on a top-mounted rail launcher.

On Jan 12 2026, Ukraine brought down a Geran-2 or Shahed UAV with Verba MPADS (man-portable air defence system).

In both cases, investigators said they found a British-made Raspberry Pi microcomputer in the drone.

When contacted, Raspberry Pi said it was aware that its products had been “diverted through third-party supply chains” and used in Russian drones.

The tech firm said that “diversion can occur via complex and opaque chains of onward sale in jurisdictions where visibility is limited”.

A spokesman for the company added: “Raspberry Pi complies fully with all applicable UK, EU and international sanctions regimes and operates controls designed to track and prevent diversion to Russia or the Russian defence sector.

“The company does not disclose the detailed mechanics of its anti-diversion processes.”

Campaigners said this was not good enough.

Samuel Bickett, a former political prisoner in Hong Kong and co-author of CFHK’s report, said: “British manufacturers of components used in Russian weapons are not doing enough to control the flow of these goods across the world.

TT Electronics in Woking, Surrey, looks like any other grey, industrial manufacturing business.

According to its website, it makes advanced electronics for “aerospace, defence, healthcare, automation and electrification”.

It has 19 manufacturing sites across Britain, China, North America and Thailand.

Ukrainian investigators say they have found 11 transistors manufactured by TT Electronics in automated radio jammers and transmitter interference devices used by the Russians on the battlefield.

Between 2022 and 2024, documents reviewed by CFHK suggest that Russia received 59 imports of TT Electronics-labelled components with a declared value of $1.2m. Around 40 per cent of these shipments, worth $700,000, went via Hong Kong.

Hong Kong has become an increasingly important hub for sanctions evasion used by foreign governments seeking to dodge Western embargoes. 

The TT Electronics shipments were imported by four Hong Kong-based traders and logistics firms, including companies associated with the electronics supplier Woeroon. 

Woeroon, which made 25,707 shipments to Russia between 2022 and 2024, is a collective of several unrelated companies. 

Sir Iain Duncan Smith, co-chairman of the Interparliamentary Alliance on China, said: “This Government is turning out to be a lapdog to China.

“It is China and the CCP-controlled Hong Kong that is allowing dual-use components into Russia.

“Sanction evasion has been going on for a while and it is absolutely wrong for the government to turn a blind eye to it.

“The British Government doesn’t seem to care when the sanctions we are meant to be imposing are not being enforced at all. It is an absolute disgrace.

“And the result is that Russia can continue fighting its brutal war of invasion.”

Golledge Electronics, headquartered in Basingstoke, also has products which appear to have been used by Russia.

The company’s “frequency control products” are said by investigators to have been repeatedly discovered inside sophisticated Russian weaponry, including the Tornado C guided missile, the 9M727 cruise missile and the Kh-47M2 Kinzhal missile.

Since Putin’s invasion in 2022, Ukrainian government investigators say they have discovered eight Golledge-labelled components in Russian weapons. Most recently, a Quartz oscillator was found in a cruise missile on Oct 25 2025.

Between 2022 and 2024, Russia received 114 Golledge Electronics-labelled shipments, 30 of which came via six Hong Kong-based companies, including Woeroon and Asialink Shanghai International Logistics Co Ltd, according to analysis by CFHK.

Asialink, a cargo transportation service which has an official partnership with Russia’s national airline Aeroflot, supplied 3,700 high-risk goods worth $6.3m to Russia from March 2022 to January 2024, according to CFHK. It even used Russian as the default language for its website in 2023. 

While the US has already sanctioned Asialink for continued shipments to businesses in Russia, the EU and UK have not.

TT Electronics and Golledge Electronics were contacted for comment.

There is no suggestion that any of the British companies named are deliberately selling their products to Russia.

Bill Browder – the American-born businessman, author and head of the Global Magnitsky Justice campaign – said: “It’s outrageous that British components would have found their way into Russian weapons. 

“This is exactly what the UK sanctions were supposed to prevent. 

“I hope that we figure out how these components found their way into these weapons so sanctions can be tightened.

“Innocent people in Ukraine have died because of these weapons being used on them and we need to get to the bottom of this.”

Chloe Cheung, a Hong Kong dissident who has a China-issued 1,000,000 HK dollar (£100,000) bounty on her head, co-wrote the CFHK report. She said: “It’s shocking that the city I grew up in is now linked to illegal weapons trade rather than to the values of integrity and accountability that once defined it.

“Hong Kong is funnelling components into Russia’s war machine. Our city is indirectly contributing to the killing of Ukrainians. This is the result of Chinese Communist Party control.

“A city once known for rule of law and clean governance, which claims to be an international financial hub, has instead become a haven for dirty money and illegal traders, barely recognisable as the place I once called home.”

Lord Austin, vice-chairman of the Magnitsky Sanctions and Reparation all-party parliamentary group, said: “It would be completely unacceptable if British components are fuelling Russia’s war machine.

“Ministers must take immediate steps to close any sanctions loopholes.”

Mr Bickett, the CFHK report's co-author, added: “It is critical that Britain work with allies to create a genuine multilateral authority with the power to institute sanctions quickly and broadly against all Russian and non-Russian parties involved in evading export controls.”

Responding to our findings, a government spokesman said: “We take reports of goods from UK companies being found in Russian weaponry incredibly seriously.

“We have banned the export of thousands of goods to Russia, including every battlefield item Ukraine has brought to our attention, and together with our international partners have implemented the most severe package of sanctions ever imposed on a major economy.

“Non-compliance with sanctions is a serious offence punishable through large financial penalties or criminal prosecution.”

Konstantin Bychek, 36, was standing by his kitchen window when a North Korean KN-23 struck the neighbouring apartment block on April 24, 2025.

“The shock wave threw me against the wall,” he said while standing in the remains of his old home, almost a year after the deadly strike.

“My face and neck were shredded by shards of glass, and I was drenched in blood.

“I shut my eyes, but behind my eyelids I could see a blinding orange flash [from] when the explosion happened.”

Natalia, his wife, who also survived the explosion, said Europe should crack down on sanctions evasion. “We would like all possible supplies to Russia to be cut off,” she said.

“I don't understand how this can happen, given that Russia is under sanctions.”

Igor, who is now his sister Yana’s legal guardian, warned: “Western Europe should not forget what is going on in Ukraine.

“If Europe doesn't help us now, you will see the same in your countries.”

As we approach the fourth anniversary of Russia’s invasion of Ukraine, we must ask ourselves: why is Europe still shooting itself?
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: AI paranoia grips the City as chatbots take over
data.description:
Amadeo Alentorn still remembers the nervous questions he was asked when he began to let computer algorithms dictate stock market trades more than 20 years ago.

“Some investors would come to us and say, ‘What? You let computers decide which stocks to buy?’” says Alentorn, the head of systematic equities at Jupiter Asset Management.

“We said, ‘Yes, that’s exactly what we do. We build investment strategies that get coded up and then computers make those decisions.’”

Algorithmic trading has become the norm today for asset managers and other professional investors. But computers could soon be taking over even more of our financial lives.

Billions have been wiped off the market value of wealth management stocks this week as investors panic that artificial intelligence (AI) chatbots and programmes will soon be able to manage people’s money.

The sector is the latest to be hit by an AI panic – with software, law and accounting all suffering similar crashes in recent days.

More than £1bn has been wiped off the value of St James’s Place, the biggest wealth manager in Britain, this week, while rivals such as Quilter also tumbled. Stockbroking platforms have also crashed, with AJ Bell also falling.

The latest turmoil was triggered on Tuesday, when Altruist, a “unicorn” Los Angeles start-up, put out a press release announcing the launch of a new AI tax-strategy planner.

The programme, called Hazel, can write personalised tax strategies in minutes and can run interactive modelling on everything from the impact of a bonus to a home sale or retirement.

Jason Wenk, Altruist’s founder, says it “expands what a single advisor can handle, raises the bar on outcomes, and makes average advice a lot harder to justify”.

The tool has triggered mass panic in the stock market.

In the US, shares in wealth management and investment companies Charles Schwab, Raymond James and Morgan Stanley all tumbled. European equivalents Julius Baer, UBS and Amundi were also hit by the blanket selling.

“When you have something as fundamental as a rewriting of the economic landscape, driven by AI, the winners will only be known in years to come,” says Martin Frandsen, a portfolio manager at Principal Asset Management.

“Right now the market is being very non-discriminatory in terms of which companies will actually be the winners and losers.”

Feverish markets

The sell-off in wealth management is just the latest tremor of a broader AI earthquake hitting stock markets.

Software companies shed $830bn (£609bn) in six days this month after Anthropic announced a new plug-in for its Claude agent that can complete tasks across data analysis, marketing, legal and sales.

In turn came new fears for the private credit sector, which has invested heavily in software companies.

On Monday, insurance stocks tumbled after OpenAI approved the first AI insurance app on ChatGPT.

On Wednesday and Thursday, property companies companies similarly saw their share prices plummet on fears bots may replace estate agents.

Even the trucking and deliver industry has been hit. Shares were sent into tailspin after Algorhythm Holdings, a former maker of karaoke systems for cars, unveiled an AI tool designed to optimise freight efficiencies. The business triggered turmoil despite having a valuation of just $6m (£4.4m).

Christian Dery, of Capital Fund Management, warns: “Firms with generic business models, low barriers to entry and high risk of automation will not survive.”

The wealth sell-off is not so much because Hazel is a seismic technological breakthrough – it’s more because markets are feverish.

“You have all the fast money hedge funds selling whoever is seen as the next AI loser straight away,” says Emmanuel Cau, the head of European equity strategy at Barclays.

Analysts think the AI tool itself is not necessarily revolutionary.

Its updated software may be able to read payslips, bank statements, meeting notes, email and customer data but it is designed to help human advisors create personalised tax strategies, not replace them.

“It is very much fear driving this rather than fundamentals,” says Cau. “The market is moving to who is the next loser, the next victim, of this AI disruption phase.

“Things are moving very fast, and it’s very hard to keep pace and a lot of investors are getting hit. It is a massive pain trade.”

AI ‘reality is dawning’

While the tool itself may not be revolutionary, investors and the industry are alarmed at the pace at which the technology is evolving.

Susannah Streeter, chief investment strategist at the Wealth Club, says: “It’s not like the warning signs haven’t been here.

“It’s just that I don’t think the wider industry really believed that they could work quite so quickly, or couldn’t really understand exactly how they would be disruptive. I think that reality is dawning right now.”

For wealth managers and advisers, the fear is AI could ultimately drive down fees, which are typically opaque and vary significantly between firms. Analysts do not think that companies will go under, but their ability to charge may be significantly degraded and that has massive implications for their share prices.

“I think it has sent a bit of a shiver through the industry, this feeling that fees may be compressed in the future, this feeling that it’s going to be a lot harder to win customers and charge substantial fees for it,” says Streeter.

Wealth managers are trying to put a brave face on it.

Speaking at UBS’s Financial Services Conference on Tuesday, Jed Finn, the head of wealth management at Morgan Stanley, said: “It is the opposite. I think AI is going to enhance the quality of advice, and it’s going to help advisors scale and be able to serve more clients more effectively with the same set of resources.”

Human element trust

City folk argue that there are limits on how far people will be willing to use ChatGPT or other AI tools to make investment decisions.

“Everyone has heard about the so-called hallucinations of large language models, that ChatGPT-type applications can sometimes give you a very incorrect answer with a lot of confidence,” says Alentorn.

“When it comes to important, potentially life-changing decisions like the deployment of capital, a catastrophic error would really have a big impact on someone’s life.”

He adds: “Clearly what’s important there is the role of the human being in creating and designing those tools and making sure that the right safeguards are in place.”

There is more to the human element than just making sure AI is fit for purpose, too.

“For all of the technological and efficiency improvements that AI has the potential to bring to the wealth industry, we don’t think it, or other automated solutions, can ever fully replace the ‘human empathy’ and ‘trust’ side of being a financial adviser,” says David McCann, an analyst at Deutsche Bank.

However, he acknowledges that “hand holding” during market downturns may be less important to “more digitally native, younger individuals” – especially as they become more confident and trusting of AI.

It is a possibility that a 25-year-old who is starting to build a portfolio today may never feel they need a human wealth manager.

“Think about all of those people who’ve already got a relationship with their chatbot. They’re asking them for relationship advice, for health advice,” says Streeter.

“It’s not a great leap to expect them to be trusting a chatbot to give them the best financial advice.”

data.title: Labour hits ferries with new net zero tax
data.description:
Northern Ireland businesses are set to be hammered by a green tax which bosses fear will make them uncompetitive.

A new tax on ferries to Northern Ireland, set to be introduced this summer, will swell freight rates – weighing on the economy and driving up prices.

The levy system, known as the emissions trading scheme (ETS), stands to increase shipping costs to the province by 6pc when it comes into force in July, according industry estimates.

The UK Chamber of Shipping said that will put more pressure on an economy where supply chain costs are already higher than the rest of the UK because of arrangements imposed under Brexit’s Windsor Framework.

It said the tax will also add to the red tape foisted on business following the agreement, which placed Northern Ireland in the EU’s single market for goods after Brexit.

The ETS scheme requires shipping firms to pay for their CO2 emissions as part of the Government’s push to reach net zero by 2050.

Unlike other ferry routes, Northern Ireland will not be exempt from the charges, even though 90pc of commercial and passenger traffic goes by sea.

The Chamber said ferry firms will most likely have to pass the additional charges on.

Stena Line, which operates ferries to Belfast from Liverpool, Heysham in Lancashire and Cairnryan in Scotland, estimates that the scheme will increase its costs by £9m annually. That would average £15 for every HGV, van and car.

The Chamber said an initial 50pc deduction to match the carbon charges on voyages between Britain and Republic of Ireland will provide little relief.

That is because exporters in Northern Ireland compete mostly with firms in Britain rather than the province’s southern neighbour, to which they will be at a structural disadvantage.

UK firms have already spent millions on some of the most sustainably designed ferries, including methanol and rotor sail propulsion, leaving no financially viable way forward, it claimed.

The Chamber said: “The sector supports the UK’s climate goals, but cannot deliver meaningful emissions reduction without the necessary fuels, infrastructure, and clear guidance in place.

“Premature implementation risks higher costs for passengers and freight, with limited environmental gain. However, the Government is pressing ahead, leaving industry in an untenable position.”

The trade group urged ministers to delay introduction of the scheme while further assessments are carried out and said the revenue raised should be ring-fenced for investment in clean fuels and quayside power connections.

The Department for Energy Security and Net Zero said that it is for ship operators to decide how much of the expense of the ETS scheme to pass on, and that consumers should not see significant costs.

A spokesman said: “Pricing emissions is a cost-effective way to cut carbon emissions and encourage greener travel. We are working closely with industry on implementation.”

data.title: Train driver who fell asleep at controls drops attempt to overturn sacking
data.description:
A train driver who fell asleep at the controls has abandoned a trade union-backed attempt to overturn his sacking.

The unnamed Hull Trains driver was dismissed a year ago after experiencing “fatigue” at the controls of the company’s 125mph services on several occasions, The Telegraph previously disclosed.

These came to light when he mentioned them in passing during a work meeting, having previously been helped through similar health-related problems by Hull Trains’ managers.

Trade union Aslef called almost a full year of strikes to try to reverse the sacking but admitted defeat on Friday, with the former driver accepting he would not be reinstated.

The series of ineffectual Aslef strikes led to union membership among Hull Trains’ drivers dropping by more than a third during the past year, The Telegraph understands.

Fewer and fewer drivers were keeping up their Aslef membership, which averaged £373 per year in 2024, even as the union claimed that its members were repeatedly voting in favour of walkouts.

In a letter from last March explaining the driver’s dismissal, Martijn Gilbert, Hull Trains’ managing director, said: “The disclosures made by this driver ... presented us with a safety risk that we could not ignore.”

It continued: “We cannot be confident that they can be trusted to properly report safety matters ... so that we can support them and manage the risk.”

Under Mick Whelan, its previous general secretary, Aslef claimed Hull Trains was spreading “false and inaccurate” information.

Aslef and other unions claimed victory over the Conservative government in 2024 in a long-running row about anti-union laws designed to limit strikes’ ability to halt rail services.

Labour has repealed that law – the Minimum Service Levels Act 2023 – as part of pro-union reforms designed to boost membership numbers within such organisations, which in turn channel some of their members’ subscriptions into the political party’s funds.

Aslef failed to respond to a request for comment.

A spokesman for Hull Trains said: “We are pleased that our customers can continue to travel with confidence when booking with Hull Trains, following the positive outcome of our talks with Aslef.

“The safety of our customers and colleagues has and always will continue to be our number one priority.

“We look forward to welcoming record numbers of customers onboard in 2026 after achieving best long-distance train operator in the rail customer experience survey published in December.”
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: Morrisons to sell dozens of pharmacies in scramble to cut costs
data.description:
Morrisons has put dozens of pharmacies up for sale in a fresh effort to cut costs.

The private equity-owned supermarket has launched a sale process after concluding that many of its in-store pharmacies are not financially viable.

As a result of the review, property agents have been tasked with selling some of the pharmacies on a store-by-store basis, rather than the entire portfolio.

Those affected are likely to remain open in stores, but under the brand of any eventual buyer.

The move to sell dozens of pharmacies comes after Morrisons’ losses plunged to £381m last year, fuelled by steep borrowing costs.

Rising debt pressures have led bosses to reduce borrowings, which fell to £3.1bn from £3.5bn last summer.

The sale of pharmacies is the latest attempt to strip back the business and focus increasingly on traditional retail.

A separate restructuring last year triggered the closure of four pharmacies, alongside 52 cafés, 17 convenience stores, 35 meat counters and 13 florists.

This led to more than 300 jobs being put at risk of redundancy, as bosses identified areas “where the costs of operations are significantly out of line with usage, volumes, or the value that customers place on them”.

Rami Baitieh, chief executive of Morrisons, said the cuts were a “necessary part of our plans to renew and reinvigorate Morrisons”.

Morrisons is pursuing a turnaround strategy after Mr Baitieh said the business had been going through a “challenging period”.

The retail boss blamed weaker consumer spending among poorer shoppers, many of whom have been affected by price rises.

This has led to Morrisons struggling to boost its share of the grocery market, which is at 8.4pc.

That is up narrowly from 8.5pc this time last year, although it is well below the 9.9pc it achieved in October 2021, when it was bought by private equity firm Clayton Dubilier & Rice in a £10bn deal.

This has prompted speculation that Morrisons could lose its position as Britain’s fifth-largest supermarket to Lidl, which now boasts 7.7pc of the grocery market.

Morrisons is not alone in seeking to sell off pharmacies, as the sector has been battered by rising costs in recent years.

Around 1,400 pharmacies have shut in England since 2017, according to the National Pharmacy Association, leaving many households unable to access medicine in their areas.

This has been blamed on rising wage costs and a shortfall in how much the NHS pays pharmacies.

Boots has also closed hundreds of pharmacies in recent years, while LloydsPharmacy, which had 138 sites, collapsed in 2023.

Rachel Reeves has piled more pressure on pharmacies over the past year by increasing business rates and the minimum wage.

Supermarkets have also come under pressure from rising taxes under Labour, most notably by the Chancellor’s decision to increase employer National Insurance rates.

Speaking last month, Mr Baitieh pleaded with the Government to avoid hitting retailers with any extra costs.

He said: “I would say, please, no more. This is very important for the market. We expect inflation to go up.”

Morrisons declined to comment.
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: Shadow banking giant blocks investors from withdrawing cash
data.description:
A Wall Street giant has blocked investors from withdrawing money from one of its flagship private credit funds as concerns mount over a crisis in the shadow banking industry.

New York investment firm Blue Owl scrapped plans to let investors take their cash from its $1.7bn (£1.3bn) fund, saying it would repay them in quarterly instalments over an extended period instead.

It marked a reversal of previous plans to let investors take money out of its Blue Owl Capital Corporation II fund, which was launched in 2017 to allow retail investors to put money into private debt markets.

Blue Owl is one of the biggest private credit businesses in the world with more than $307bn worth of investments including in 20 Asda supermarkets in the UK, which it acquired for $467m last November.

Its Blue Owl Capital Corporation II fund, or OBDC II, mostly invests in middle-market companies in the US and has given loans to more than 180 businesses.

Blue Owl’s sudden reversal follows growing concerns about the private credit industry after the collapse of two heavily indebted US companies resulted in lenders suffering billions of dollars in losses.

The bankruptcies of First Brands and Tricolor caused shockwaves across the private credit industry as lenders ranging from Santander to BlackRock found themselves exposed.

It led to concerns about a lack of transparency in private debt markets with Jamie Dimon, the chief executive of JP Morgan, warning that investors would probably see more “cockroaches” emerge across the shadow banking system.

Blue Owl first stopped investors from withdrawing money from its OBDC II fund last November after scrapping plans to merge it into the larger, publicly traded OBDC fund, which controls $17bn worth of investments.

As an alternative, Blue Owl had planned to host quarterly tender offers where investors could withdraw cash from the OBDC II fund by selling stakes at its stated value at the time. However, Blue Owl is no longer going ahead with those plans.

The OBDC II fund is currently privately owned by Blue Owl, meaning investors are unable to sell their holdings on the stock market.

Blue Owl said it was now expecting to sell the remainder of its assets over the coming years and return money to investors on a quarterly basis.

The announcement was made as Blue Owl said it had struck an agreement to sell $600m worth of OBDC II’s loan book as part of a wider deal worth $1.4bn.

The New York firm said it would use the money raised from the sell-off to return 30pc of the money investors put into the OBDC II fund this March.

A spokesman for Blue Owl said the distribution “will provide OBDC II shareholders with significant liquidity and reflects our continued commitment to deliver value to OBDC II shareholders”.

“The overall sale underscores the strong demand for Blue Owl‑originated assets and reflects the rigour of Blue Owl Credit’s underwriting, portfolio construction and valuation processes.”
