data.title: Higher water bills are a shameless reward for failure
data.description:
On the face of it, the news that water suppliers won’t be allowed to ramp up bills by as much as they were hoping is cause for celebration.

At first glance, it looks like an all-too-rare rebuff for an industry that has been thumbing its nose at customers for far too long – which is, of course, precisely how this latest sorry charade is meant to be interpreted.

Ministers are desperate for us all to think that after decades of wrongdoing, it has taken a Labour government to finally get tough with the cowboys that pollute our rivers and charge us a fortune for the privilege, all while absentee shareholders and negligent executives line their own pockets with money that could’ve been spent on investment.

Yet, that isn’t what is happening here. Not even close.

Yes, bills won’t be increasing to the extent that suppliers were asking for – but the point is that an independent panel of “experts” has still sanctioned further rises beyond what Ofwat had approved and most objective observers thought those rises were already pretty steep.

So the idea that this is somehow a big victory for households and a defeat for water companies is profound nonsense. The opposite is true.

In fact, it is impossible to see further bill hikes as anything other than a reward for failure for an industry that has long treated customers and the environment with complete contempt.

Not that Emma Hardy, the minister for water and flooding, seems even remotely prepared to acknowledge what is really happening here.

“We are laser-focused on helping ease the cost-of-living pressure on households. We’ve frozen fuel duty, raised the minimum wage and pensions and brought down mortgage rates, putting more money in people’s pockets,” she said – though hopefully not with a straight face.

This is gaslighting territory. It takes a special kind of shamelessness to boast about making people better off at the precise moment that millions of families are having a fresh round of bill rises imposed on them.

The Competition and Markets Authority (CMA) said on Thursday it is allowing five water companies – Anglian Water, Northumbrian Water, Southern Water, South East Water and Wessex Water – to lift bills by up to 5pc over the next five years.

That might seem fairly tame if it wasn’t for the fact that it comes on top of an average increase of 24pc that industry regulator Ofwat had already approved in April.

As Mike Keil, chief executive of the Consumer Council for Water (CCW), pointed out: “Many customers are still trying to absorb the impact of April’s unprecedented rise in water bills, so any further increases will be very unwelcome.”

In fact, two in five of the households these companies serve say they will struggle to afford the increases coming down their pipe, according to the CCW, which represents water customers across England and Wales.

What’s more, the CCW has conducted its own analysis showing there was an opportunity to reduce these five companies’ financing costs to cut bills by around £41 a year – “but instead the CMA has chosen to increase their rate of return,” Keil said.

However, you would’ve thought otherwise when reading Hardy’s hollow words on Thursday.

“I understand the public anger over bill rises – that’s why I expect every water company to offer proper support to anyone struggling to pay,” she said.

But as Keil said: “Three of these five companies also have no plans to end water poverty by 2030.”

The CMA doesn’t come out of this much better. It makes a big song and dance about how the additional funding will ensure “essential improvements”, such as more resilient supply and a reduction in pollution, are delivered “at reasonable cost”.

Most people will accept some level of inflation in their household expenses but I’m willing to bet that few regard a 27pc surge as reasonable – even if it is over five years.

Some customers are already grappling with rises close to, or more than, 50pc once inflation is added because suppliers have been outrageously front-loading increases to help pay for long-overdue infrastructure improvements.

The reality is that bill-payers are being asked to subsidise an era of playing catch-up because water suppliers failed in the past to keep pace with investment, maintenance and environmental duties.

The money was there but instead of being spent modernising the system, repairing antiquated pipes and ensuring sewage wasn’t pouring into our waterways, it was being lavished on greedy investors and weak-kneed bosses or vanishing straight out the door in the form of heavy debt repayments.

Research from the University of Greenwich has found that 35p in every pound spent on customer bills is swallowed up in debt and dividend costs.

With the system buckling, it is surely no coincidence that these are the highest bill increases since privatisation.

It is also no surprise that the suppliers that went crying to the CMA are also among the most debt-laden in a sector that has long been leveraged up to the gills.

Meanwhile, the competition policeman quietly concedes that alongside a much-trumpeted round of additional investment, bill increases will also help pay down debt and boost returns for investors – many of whom are the same faceless funds that presided over the current wretched state of affairs in the first place.

For all the talk of long overdue reform and a new era of genuine accountability from Labour, it’s hard to avoid the conclusion that our broken water industry has effectively been handed a huge taxpayer-funded bailout.
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: Reeves accused of putting vital South West rail route at risk
data.description:
Rachel Reeves has been accused of putting South West England’s main rail route at risk by limiting maintenance spending while promising billions for Northern projects.

Fears are mounting that a sea cliff could collapse on to the line in Devon, cutting off Plymouth and the whole of Cornwall, after recent storms saw a sinkhole near the railway in Dawlish.

The cliff in question had been earmarked for £400m of earthworks after a 2014 collapse closed the line for eight weeks, costing the local economy £1.2bn.

However, the plan was abandoned after Labour sought to cut costs across the UK’s rail network. Instead, ministers suggested the cliff would be monitored for signs of movement. 

Storm Ingrid, which battered Devon two weeks ago, also saw waves destroy sections of a sea wall protecting the tracks at Dawlish, closing the line for two days.

Rebecca Smith, Tory MP for South West Devon, said the damage had stoked concerns that spending cuts had left the lifeline route exposed to potential disaster.

She said: “With all the tightening of the budget by the Chancellor, she has taken away contingency funding. So if there were something that needed an emergency reaction, the implication is that the money isn’t actually there.

“If you get the right kind of freakish storm, who knows what could happen. The question for me is, should this not be something that is in capital funding on a year-on-year basis?”

After Ms Smith asked in the Commons whether funding could be guaranteed for unplanned resilience work, James Murray, the chief secretary to the Treasury, said that budgets allow ministers to respond to emergencies.

He said: “All individual departments must recognise that unexpected pressures will come along; they need to prepare for them and make sure they have robust plans to respond.”

Ms Smith, a member of the transport select committee, said the Government’s reluctance to fund work on the cliff stood in contrast to its backing for the £45bn Northern Powerhouse Rail project, for which Ms Reeves last month announced £1bn of preparatory funding.

She said: “Why do they want to take a gamble when they are investing billions in other parts of the country? Why do we need a marginally faster rail system in the North when in the Southwest, if this went again, we’d be cut off for, I don’t know how long?”

Ms Smith also raised doubts about the level of monitoring that Network Rail is carrying out on the cliff, which is protected by the railway from the sea but prone to becoming sodden with surface water.

She said: “Are they monitoring it or just draining things? We need answers if they are not prepared to put the investment in to fix it.”

Martin Wrigley, Liberal Democrat MP for Newton Abbot, which includes Dawlish, said that had Storm Ingrid hit when seas were higher, the damage could have been much more severe.

He said the Department for Transport (DfT) should grant Network Rail money to undertake a study into a long-term solution for the cliff.

He said: “Monitoring is no good because the last collapse was a single, catastrophic failure, almost a tectonic shift of part of the cliff which came down on to the railway.”

Mr Wrigley said the damage to the Dawlish sea wall was also concerning, having affected areas on either side of a new section installed after the 2014 collapse.

The DfT said work carried out after the 2014 events held up well in the recent storms, with damage limited to a low wall between the coastal path and the railway. 

It said funding had been provided for a drainage trial and a range of advanced monitoring equipment, including tilt sensors that would provide a quick response if triggered.

Work will continue with Network Rail to assess the investment needed to ensure the line’s resilience and to develop any longer-term solutions.
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: Heathrow boss: Airport isn’t crowded... foreigners just walk the wrong way
data.description:
The chief executive of Heathrow has said the airport is not crowded because foreigners just walk the wrong way.

Thomas Woldbye said people complain the airport is too busy when in fact people are just “crashing into each other”.

This is because European travellers are used to walking on the right, while the British walk on the left.

Mr Woldbye told an Aviation Club UK event on Thursday: “One of the reasons people think [Terminal 5] is crowded – it’s not, actually. But yes, people are in the wrong place.

“Problem is that all the British people keep to the left and normally Europeans keep to the right. And they do that in both directions. So we can be crashing into each other and I see that from personal experience.

“We just [need to] make sure that everybody going this way keeps going left, and [those] going this way keep to the right,” he added, gesturing with his hands to illustrate the different directions.

The humorous aside came after the Danish-born airport boss warned earlier this week that Heathrow could lose its coveted spot as Europe’s busiest airport to rivals such as Amsterdam or Paris unless a third runway is approved for construction.

Mr Woldbye said on Tuesday: “We remain Europe’s largest airport but latest figures show we may lose that position in 2026 and we cannot keep driving growth for the UK economy without more capacity.”

When asked what he thought of the Government’s position on the third runway, he said it was broadly supportive. But he added: “We don’t have long-term regulation, which is clear in terms of us building a business case.”

Last year, Heidi Alexander, the Transport Secretary, gave the go-ahead to Heathrow’s preferred plan to build a third runway over the top of the M25 motorway, a scheme likely to generate controversy over its cost and complexity.

The third runway scheme, which will see a 3,500-metre strip built to the north-west of Heathrow’s existing site, is currently estimated to cost around £49bn.

Included in the controversial plans are a scheme to build a 66,000-space car park at a cost of £2.6bn, as revealed by the City AM financial newspaper last summer.

The airport boss was also criticised on Thursday for raising drop-off fees paid by drivers to £7 – and threatening an £80 fine for motorists spending longer than 10 minutes saying goodbye to their loved ones.

He said in response to the criticism: “So when we make profits on all parts of the business, rather than just running the airport, that actually goes to reducing the charge [applied to passengers’ tickets].

“Whenever we make less money ... passengers are actually paying [for] that,” he added.

“We have an incentive to make as much money as we can, on the side, from anything that actually reduces the charge for passengers.”

Heathrow’s charges are regulated by the Government. The airport pays for its infrastructure by imposing a charge on airline passengers’ tickets.

Airlines, including Virgin Atlantic and British Airways, have set up a pressure group called Heathrow Reimagined, which calls for “urgent and fundamental reform” of this funding system.
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: US is best place to invest, says boss of British drugs giant
data.description:
The boss of Britain’s largest drug developer has praised the US as the best place to invest amid fears over the waning appeal of the UK.

Dame Emma Walmsley, the outgoing chief executive of GlaxoSmithKline (GSK), said it was delusional to suggest Britain could become a competitor to the US. She lauded America as key to the group’s future growth.

“It’s more than half of GSK’s global turnover today,” she said of the US. “It’s still the leading market in the world in terms of the launches of new drugs and vaccines. It’s still, probably alongside China … the best market in the world to do business development.

“No one should be deluded that the UK is going to be a massive scale market, a domestic market, but it can be an exporter of innovation in life sciences.”

Dame Emma said GSK, which spends £1bn developing new drugs in the UK every year, is preparing to invest $30bn (£23bn) in the US by the end of this decade.

She is set to leave GSK at the end of the year after eight years in charge.

‘Worst country in Europe’ for drug prices

Her comments are likely to fuel fears about the attractiveness of Britain’s pharma sector after several major drugmakers cancelled UK projects worth billions – amid concerns over NHS drug price rules and pressure from Donald Trump, the US president.

AstraZeneca recently announced it was pausing a planned £200m investment in a Cambridge research facility, while Merck scrapped plans for a £1bn research centre in London and laid off 125 scientists.

In September, Eli Lilly, the American manufacturer of weight loss drug Mounjaro, also said that it was reconsidering plans for Gateway Labs, its London-based hub that provides support for biotech startups.

The UK Government has struggled to revive overseas interest in Britain in recent years, with the number of foreign direct investment projects falling 12pc last year to a record low of 1,375, according to official figures.

High energy costs, recent pressures like the increase in employers’ National Insurance contributions and wider geopolitical uncertainty have all been cited as reasons for declining investment in the UK.

One of the key concerns of Britain’s pharmaceuticals industry has been over NHS drug pricing in the UK. 

After postponing its investment, Dave Ricks, the chief executive of Eli Lilly, said the UK was “probably the worst country in Europe” for drug prices.

The Government agreed to a deal with the US at the start of the month to scrap tariffs on UK drug shipments – in a bid to end the row with Britain’s pharmaceutical industry. 

Dame Emma welcomed the deal as “a step in the right direction” for Britain.

The agreement means the UK will pay more for medicines through the NHS, in return for a guarantee that US import taxes on pharmaceuticals made in Britain will remain at zero for three years.
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: BBC mast operator in crisis over terrestrial TV switch-off
data.description:
The company behind Britain’s terrestrial TV and radio network has been plunged into crisis amid uncertainty over a looming digital switchover.

The future of Arqiva, which operates around 1,500 masts used by the BBC and other broadcasters, has been thrown into doubt after its largest shareholder warned its investment in the company would likely be written down to zero.

In an update to the stock market this week, Digital 9 Infrastructure said its stake of almost 52pc in Arqiva is now thought to be worth nothing.

The writedown comes as the BBC and fellow public service broadcasters ITV and Channel 4 are locked in discussions with ministers about when terrestrial TV signals, delivered via Freeview, will be switched off and replaced by streaming.

The broadcasters are keen to push ahead with the switch-off, arguing that they face huge costs to maintain energy-intensive terrestrial signals as viewers increasingly shift to streaming.

Arqiva’s network of masts reaches 98.5pc of the UK population through Freeview.

It includes the 319-metre-tall, Grade II-listed Emley Moor transmitting station, which is the tallest freestanding structure in the UK, and Lancashire’s Winter Hill, which beams TV signals to more than 2.7 million homes in areas including Manchester and Liverpool.

Under current legislation, traditional TV is slated to continue until at least 2034. However, campaigners have called for this deadline to be extended, warning that a hasty switch-off could leave older and vulnerable audiences behind.

The campaign is heavily backed by Arqiva, which has a significant commercial interest in extending the life of traditional broadcast networks.

Exposure to ‘binary events’

Ministers this week kicked off a separate review into whether FM radio signals should also be switched off in the 2030s. The commercial radio industry had previously warned that the sector was being overlooked in discussions about switching off terrestrial masts.

Digital 9 acknowledged that Arqiva was “exposed to several binary events, including government policy and regulatory decisions”.

In an update to the stock market this week, Digital 9 Infrastructure said its stake of almost 52pc in Arqiva, which was once worth £213m, is now thought to be worth nothing.

Arqiva’s tumbling value had already been signalled in November when Australian investment giant Macquarie sold its 26.5pc stake for £16.5m, giving the company a valuation of just £62m.

Digital 9 has maxed out its debt facilities, raising questions over its ability to raise more money in the event of a future crisis.

A spokesman for Arqiva said Digital 9’s valuation of its business related to the “specific to the structure of their investment”.

The added: “Arqiva is a stable business with a healthy order book of existing business (£2.8bn as at 30 June 2025) and excellent targeted growth opportunities.”

Discussions over the future of terrestrial TV have proved to be politically fraught as officials in Whitehall seek to manage the transition to streaming without leaving audiences behind.

A report by Exeter University found that while 95pc of UK homes will use TV services delivered over the internet by 2040, 5pc will still rely on terrestrial TV. The report found that these people were more likely to be female, older and from lower socioeconomic backgrounds.

In addition to the switch-off, Arqiva is gearing up for the renewal of its broadcasting contract with the BBC, which is expected next year.

It comes as the BBC also enters negotiations over the future of the licence fee, with ministers considering alternative funding models including subscriptions and advertising.

The BBC is grappling with a squeeze on its budget following a freeze in licence fee income and as more households choose not to pay the levy. Corporation bosses yesterday told staff that further cost-cutting of up to £600m is now planned over the next three years.

While ITV and Channel 4 are commercially funded, they are also grappling with a drop in revenues as viewers defect to streaming rivals such as Netflix. ITV is in negotiations over a £1.6bn takeover by Sky, though talks have begun to slow, Reuters reported on Thursday.

Digital 9 warned that its stake is worthless because the value of the investment has fallen below the level of debt it took on to acquire the shareholding from the Canada Pension Plan. Digital 9 insisted there was a “credible pathway to value in certain scenarios”.

Shonil Chande, an analyst at Panmure Liberum, said the fact that Digital 9 had “substantially overpaid” for Arqiva was “no secret”.
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: Warner Bros gives Paramount a week to submit ‘best and final offer’
data.description:
Warner Bros has given Paramount a week to table its “best and final” takeover offer, raising the prospect of a fresh bidding war with Netflix.

The Hollywood studio, which has already struck an $83bn (£61bn) deal with Netflix, said it had agreed with the streaming giant to give Paramount until Feb 23 to improve on its rival $108bn bid.

Warner Bros said its board continued to back the Netflix deal and said a shareholder vote on the takeover will take place on March 20.

However, the move signals a softening of the company’s position after it rebuffed repeated approaches from Paramount, which is controlled by Larry Ellison, the billionaire Oracle founder.

David Zaslav, the chief executive of Warner Bros Discovery, said the studio was engaging with Paramount to determine whether it could address the “deficiencies” in its previous offers and present an “actionable, binding proposal”.

Warner Bros said the change of course came after a “senior representative” for Paramount told one of its board members that the rival suitor could increase its bid from $30 to $31 per share.

They added that $31 per share would not be Paramount’s “best and final” offer, according to Warner Bros, which is currently valued at $28 a share. Netflix’s initial deal was struck at $27.75 a share.

It comes after Paramount last week launched a fresh attempt to derail Netflix’s swoop on the Hollywood studio behind the Harry Potter franchise.

The US media group offered to pay a so-called “ticking fee” of $0.25 per share to investors every quarter should its deal fail to close by the end of the year.

It also agreed to cover the $2.8bn termination fee Warner Bros would have to pay Netflix if it abandons their agreed deal, as well as $1.5bn in fees linked to Warner Bros’ debt refinancing.

Under the terms of its deal, Netflix has the right to match any improved bid tabled by Paramount.

Risk of monopoly

Paramount, led by Larry Ellison’s son David Ellison, is seeking to highlight the regulatory uncertainty surrounding Netflix’s bid.

The US Department of Justice (DoJ) has launched a competition review into the takeover amid concerns that merging two of the largest US streaming services – Netflix and Warner’s HBO Max – could hand the combined company monopoly power.

Ted Sarandos, Netflix’s co-chief executive, has played down competition concerns.

The company is planning to tell regulators that by bundling Netflix and HBO Max together, it will bring down overall streaming costs for consumers. Nevertheless, Netflix has admitted it could face up to 18 months of regulatory scrutiny.

Paramount, which, unlike Netflix, is seeking to buy Warner Bros’ cable channels including CNN, would likely also face regulatory scrutiny for its proposed deal. However, bosses have argued its approach offers greater certainty.

Netflix said Paramount had “repeatedly mischaracterised the regulatory review process by suggesting its proposal will sail through, misleading WBD stockholders about the real risk of their regulatory challenges around the world”.

It added: “WBD stockholders should not be misled into thinking that Paramount Skydance has an easier or faster path to regulatory approval – it does not.”

Paramount and Netflix have been contacted for comment.
data.author: The telegraph
